title: NOx Trap Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The NOx adsorber catalyst on bank 1 is not reducing nitrogen oxide emissions to the expected level.
fullDescription: The PCM has determined that the lean NOx trap (LNT) on bank 1 is operating below its minimum efficiency threshold. The NOx trap stores nitrogen oxides during lean-burn operation and periodically purges them during brief rich excursions. When the downstream NOx sensor detects that the trap is no longer effectively converting NOx, this code is set. This is primarily found on diesel and lean-burn gasoline engines equipped with NOx aftertreatment systems.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter reduced power mode and will fail emissions testing. Prolonged driving may cause further catalyst degradation.

causes.cause: Degraded or contaminated NOx adsorber catalyst
Faulty downstream NOx sensor providing incorrect readings
Exhaust leak upstream of the NOx trap reducing efficiency
Incorrect regeneration strategy due to PCM software fault
Sulfur contamination from high-sulfur fuel

symptoms: Reduced engine power or limp mode activation
Increased fuel consumption during frequent regeneration attempts
Sulfur or rotten egg smell from exhaust

fixSteps.title: Scan and Inspect
fixSteps.instruction: Connect an OBD-II scanner and record all stored codes, freeze frame data, and NOx sensor readings. Check for related codes such as P2001, P2200, or P2201.

fixSteps.title: Test NOx Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Using a scan tool capable of reading NOx sensor values, compare upstream and downstream NOx sensor readings during a drive cycle. Upstream should show high NOx levels while downstream should be significantly lower if the trap is working.

fixSteps.title: Check for Exhaust Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the exhaust system between the engine and the NOx trap for any leaks at gaskets, flanges, or cracked pipes. Use a smoke machine if necessary to detect small leaks.

fixSteps.title: Attempt Desulfation Procedure
fixSteps.instruction: If sulfur contamination is suspected, perform a forced desulfation using the scan tool to command extended high-temperature regeneration. Monitor exhaust temps to ensure they reach the required 650-700C range.

fixSteps.title: Replace NOx Trap or Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If efficiency does not improve after desulfation, replace the NOx adsorber catalyst. If sensor readings were incorrect, replace the faulty NOx sensor. Clear codes and perform a full drive cycle to verify the repair.

faq.q: Can I drive with a P2000 code?
faq.a: You can drive short distances, but the vehicle may enter reduced power mode. Prolonged driving with a failing NOx trap increases emissions significantly and may cause the catalyst to degrade further.

faq.q: Does P2000 only affect diesel vehicles?
faq.a: No, it can also appear on lean-burn gasoline engines that use NOx trap aftertreatment, though it is more common on diesel-powered vehicles.

faq.q: Can bad fuel cause this code?
faq.a: Yes. High-sulfur fuel can contaminate the NOx adsorber catalyst, reducing its efficiency. Using ultra-low sulfur diesel (ULSD) or top-tier gasoline helps prevent this.

title: Diesel Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The diesel particulate filter on bank 1 is not trapping soot particles efficiently enough to meet emissions standards.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the diesel particulate filter (DPF) on bank 1 is not operating at the required efficiency level. The DPF captures soot particles from diesel exhaust and periodically burns them off during regeneration cycles. When the differential pressure sensor or downstream particulate matter sensor indicates insufficient filtration, this code is stored. A failed DPF can result from excessive ash loading, physical damage, or failed regeneration cycles.
safetyImpact: Vehicle may enter limp mode with significantly reduced power. Continued driving risks DPF meltdown from excessive soot buildup.

causes.cause: DPF clogged with excessive soot due to failed regeneration cycles
Cracked or melted DPF substrate allowing soot to pass through
Faulty differential pressure sensor giving false readings
Excessive oil consumption coating the DPF with ash
Incorrect fuel injection timing preventing proper regeneration

symptoms: Check Engine Light or DPF warning light illuminated
Reduced engine power or limp mode
Frequent regeneration attempts with increased exhaust temperature

fixSteps.title: Read Codes and DPF Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use an advanced OBD-II scanner to read DPF soot loading percentage, ash loading, exhaust backpressure, and regeneration history. Note how many regeneration attempts have occurred and whether they completed successfully.

fixSteps.title: Check Differential Pressure Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the DPF differential pressure sensor and its hoses for blockage, damage, or disconnection. Test sensor output with the scanner while the engine is running at various RPMs.

fixSteps.title: Attempt Forced Regeneration
fixSteps.instruction: Using a compatible scan tool, initiate a forced DPF regeneration. Ensure the vehicle is in a safe, well-ventilated area as exhaust temperatures will exceed 600C. Monitor soot loading before and after to verify the regeneration succeeded.

fixSteps.title: Professional DPF Cleaning or Replacement
fixSteps.instruction: If forced regeneration fails, the DPF may need professional thermal cleaning or chemical flushing to remove accumulated ash and soot. If the substrate is physically damaged, replace the DPF assembly. Clear codes and complete a full drive cycle.

faq.q: Can I just remove the DPF to fix this?
faq.a: Removing the DPF is illegal in many jurisdictions under the Clean Air Act and similar regulations. It will also cause the vehicle to fail emissions inspections and may trigger additional fault codes.

faq.q: Why does my DPF keep clogging?
faq.a: Frequent short trips that do not allow the exhaust to reach regeneration temperatures are the most common cause. Extended highway driving periodically helps the DPF regenerate naturally.

faq.q: How long does a DPF last?
faq.a: A properly maintained DPF typically lasts 100,000 to 150,000 miles. Ash accumulation from engine oil is the primary life-limiting factor and cannot be burned off during regeneration.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Open (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps on bank 1 are stuck in the open position.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the intake manifold runner control valve or flaps on bank 1 are not responding correctly and remain in the open position. The IMRC system adjusts the length or cross-section of the intake manifold runners to optimize airflow at different engine speeds. At low RPM, shorter runners (flaps closed) improve torque, while at high RPM, longer runners (flaps open) improve power. When the flaps are stuck open, low-end torque and throttle response suffer.
safetyImpact: Reduced low-end torque may affect vehicle responsiveness during city driving and merging maneuvers.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup on IMRC flaps preventing closure
Failed IMRC actuator motor or solenoid
Broken or disconnected IMRC linkage or shaft
Faulty IMRC position sensor
Damaged wiring or connector to the IMRC actuator

symptoms: Reduced low-RPM torque and sluggish acceleration
Audible fluttering or rattling from the intake manifold

fixSteps.title: Scan and Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Connect an OBD-II scanner and verify P2004. Use the scanner to command the IMRC actuator and observe whether the flaps move. Listen for actuator motor activation.

fixSteps.title: Inspect IMRC Flaps and Linkage
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake manifold top cover if accessible and visually inspect the IMRC flaps. Check for carbon buildup, broken linkage pins, or cracked flaps. Move flaps by hand to test for free movement.

fixSteps.title: Clean Carbon Deposits
fixSteps.instruction: If carbon buildup is preventing flap closure, use intake cleaner and a nylon brush to carefully remove deposits from the flaps and their pivot points. Avoid damaging the flap surfaces.

fixSteps.title: Test and Replace Actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Use a multimeter to check the IMRC actuator motor for proper resistance (typically 5-15 ohms). Apply 12V directly to test operation. If the actuator is faulty, replace it. Clear codes and test drive.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with P2004?
faq.a: Yes, the vehicle is generally safe to drive. You will experience reduced low-end torque and possibly slightly worse fuel economy, but there is no immediate safety risk.

faq.q: What is the intake manifold runner control?
faq.a: The IMRC system uses moveable flaps inside the intake manifold to change the effective runner length. This optimizes the air charge at different engine speeds for better torque and power across the RPM range.

faq.q: Can carbon cleaning fix this permanently?
faq.a: Carbon cleaning often resolves the immediate issue, but if the underlying cause of excessive carbon buildup is not addressed (such as PCV system issues or oil consumption), the problem will recur.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Closed (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner control flaps on bank 1 are stuck in the closed position.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the intake manifold runner control (IMRC) valve flaps on bank 1 remain in the closed position and do not open when commanded. With the flaps stuck closed, the shorter intake runner path is always active, which restricts airflow at higher RPMs and reduces top-end power output. This code is the counterpart to P2004 (stuck open) and shares similar root causes.
safetyImpact: Reduced high-RPM power output may affect overtaking and highway merging ability.

causes.cause: Carbon deposits preventing IMRC flaps from opening
Seized or failed IMRC actuator motor
Broken IMRC shaft or linkage arm
Corroded or damaged wiring to the IMRC actuator

symptoms: Noticeable loss of power at higher RPMs
Engine feels restricted or breathless above mid-range RPM
Slightly reduced fuel economy at highway speeds
Possible whistling noise from intake at high RPM

fixSteps.title: Diagnostic Scan
fixSteps.instruction: Read all stored and pending codes. Use bi-directional scanner controls to command the IMRC flaps open and confirm they do not respond.

fixSteps.title: Physical Inspection of IMRC System
fixSteps.instruction: Access the IMRC flaps through the intake manifold. Attempt to move the flaps by hand to check for binding. Inspect the actuator linkage for broken or disconnected components.

fixSteps.title: Clean or Free Stuck Flaps
fixSteps.instruction: Apply intake cleaner to the flap pivot points and carefully work the flaps back and forth to free them. Remove heavy carbon deposits with a plastic scraper or nylon brush to avoid scratching the manifold bore.

fixSteps.title: Replace IMRC Actuator or Manifold
fixSteps.instruction: If the actuator motor has failed or the shaft is broken, replace the actuator. In some designs where the flaps are integral to the manifold, the entire intake manifold assembly may need replacement. Clear codes and test drive.

faq.q: Will P2006 cause my engine to stall?
faq.a: No, P2006 should not cause stalling. The flaps stuck closed means the engine uses the shorter runner path at all times, which actually improves low-RPM performance while hurting high-RPM power.

faq.q: Can I drive long-term with this code?
faq.a: While not immediately dangerous, driving long-term may result in reduced fuel economy at highway speeds and limited top-end power. It is best to address the issue within a few weeks.

faq.q: Is this related to the throttle body?
faq.a: No, the IMRC system is separate from the throttle body. The throttle body controls total airflow, while the IMRC adjusts the intake runner geometry to optimize engine breathing at different RPMs.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit Open (Bank 1)
shortDescription: An open circuit has been detected in the intake manifold runner control actuator wiring on bank 1.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit condition in the electrical circuit controlling the intake manifold runner control (IMRC) actuator on bank 1. This means the PCM cannot communicate with or command the IMRC actuator due to a break in the wiring, a disconnected connector, or a failed actuator motor coil. Without electrical control, the IMRC flaps will remain in their default position.
safetyImpact: Reduced engine performance at certain RPM ranges. No immediate safety hazard but merging and overtaking may be affected.

causes.cause: Disconnected or corroded IMRC actuator connector
Broken wire in the IMRC actuator harness
Failed IMRC actuator motor with open coil winding
Blown fuse in the IMRC control circuit
PCM driver circuit failure

symptoms: Reduced engine power at certain RPM ranges
No audible IMRC actuator movement during RPM changes

fixSteps.title: Check Fuse and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the IMRC actuator fuse in the engine bay fuse box and test it with a test light or multimeter. Then locate the IMRC actuator connector and check that it is fully seated and free of corrosion.

fixSteps.title: Test Circuit Continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the IMRC actuator connector and use a multimeter to check continuity on each wire from the connector back to the PCM. Consult the wiring diagram for correct pin identification. An open reading indicates a broken wire.

fixSteps.title: Test IMRC Actuator Motor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the resistance across the IMRC actuator motor terminals. Compare to manufacturer specifications (typically 5-15 ohms). An infinite reading confirms an open motor coil.

fixSteps.title: Repair Wiring or Replace Actuator
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any broken wires using solder and heat-shrink tubing. Replace corroded connectors with weatherproof replacements. If the actuator motor is open-circuit, replace the actuator assembly. Clear codes and verify the repair.

faq.q: What is the difference between P2008 and P2004?
faq.a: P2004 indicates the IMRC flaps are physically stuck open, while P2008 indicates an electrical open circuit in the wiring to the actuator. P2008 is an electrical fault; P2004 is a mechanical or position fault.

faq.q: Could a rodent have caused this?
faq.a: Yes, rodent damage to wiring harnesses is a common cause of open circuit codes, including P2008. Inspect the harness carefully for chew marks, especially in areas near the firewall or along the top of the engine.

faq.q: Will this cause any engine damage?
faq.a: No, this code will not cause engine damage. The engine will simply operate with the IMRC in its default position, resulting in non-optimal performance at certain RPM ranges.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner position sensor on bank 1 is providing an out-of-range or irrational signal.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a fault in the circuit of the intake manifold runner position sensor or switch on bank 1. This sensor provides feedback to the PCM about the actual position of the IMRC flaps. When the sensor signal does not match expected values or is outside the normal voltage range, this code is set. The PCM uses this feedback to verify that the IMRC flaps have moved to the commanded position.
safetyImpact: Minimal safety impact. Engine performance may be slightly reduced due to the PCM defaulting the IMRC to a safe position.

causes.cause: Faulty IMRC position sensor or switch
Corroded or damaged sensor connector
Chafed or broken sensor signal wire
IMRC flaps not fully reaching commanded position

symptoms: IMRC system may default to a fixed position

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use an OBD-II scanner to monitor the IMRC position sensor voltage or duty cycle in real time. Compare the reading to specifications for both open and closed flap positions.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the IMRC position sensor on the intake manifold. Inspect the connector for corrosion, bent pins, or loose fit. Wiggle the connector while monitoring live data for intermittent faults.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: With the connector disconnected, measure the sensor resistance and compare to specifications. Check the reference voltage (typically 5V) and ground at the connector using a multimeter.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty position sensor. If wiring damage is found, repair using proper automotive-grade connectors and solder joints. Clear codes and verify proper sensor readings across the IMRC operating range.

faq.q: Is P2014 serious?
faq.a: P2014 is generally a low-severity code. The engine will run but may not have optimal performance across all RPM ranges. It should still be repaired to maintain proper engine tuning.

faq.q: Can I ignore this code?
faq.a: While not dangerous, ignoring P2014 means the PCM cannot verify IMRC flap position, which may mask more serious IMRC mechanical failures. Address it at your next scheduled maintenance.

faq.q: Where is the IMRC position sensor located?
faq.a: The sensor is typically mounted on the side or top of the intake manifold near the IMRC actuator. Consult your vehicle-specific service manual for the exact location.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner position sensor on bank 1 is reporting values outside the expected performance range.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the intake manifold runner position sensor on bank 1 is providing a signal that does not correlate with the commanded IMRC position or changes too slowly or erratically. Unlike P2014 which indicates a general circuit fault, P2015 specifically flags a range or performance issue, meaning the sensor is connected and producing a signal, but that signal is not within expected parameters during IMRC operation.
safetyImpact: Moderate impact on engine performance. The PCM may disable IMRC operation entirely, reducing efficiency across the RPM range.

causes.cause: Worn or intermittent IMRC position sensor
IMRC flaps binding or sticking, not reaching full travel
Loose or worn IMRC actuator linkage causing sensor disagreement
High resistance in sensor signal wire causing voltage drop

symptoms: Reduced engine power at various RPMs

fixSteps.title: Monitor Live Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to observe the IMRC position sensor reading while commanding the actuator through its full range. Note any dead spots, erratic jumps, or slow response in the sensor signal.

fixSteps.title: Verify Mechanical IMRC Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Manually move the IMRC flaps through their full range while watching the sensor output. If the flaps bind or stop short of full travel, carbon buildup or mechanical damage is the root cause rather than the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Wiring Integrity
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the resistance of the signal and ground wires from the sensor connector to the PCM. Any resistance above 1-2 ohms indicates a high-resistance connection that could cause range/performance issues.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair IMRC Mechanism
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor output is erratic even with good wiring and free-moving flaps, replace the sensor. If the flaps are binding, clean or repair the IMRC mechanism. Clear codes and verify smooth sensor tracking through the full range.

faq.q: What is the difference between P2014 and P2015?
faq.a: P2014 indicates a general circuit fault (open, short, or no signal), while P2015 indicates the sensor is providing a signal but it is outside the expected range or performance parameters.

faq.q: Can a dirty intake manifold cause P2015?
faq.a: Yes, carbon buildup inside the intake manifold can restrict IMRC flap movement, causing the position sensor to report values that do not match the commanded position.

faq.q: Is it common on any specific vehicles?
faq.a: P2015 is particularly common on Volkswagen and Audi vehicles with the 2.0L TDI engine, where the intake manifold flap motor and position sensor are known failure points.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner position sensor signal voltage on bank 1 is lower than the minimum expected value.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the signal voltage from the intake manifold runner position sensor on bank 1 is below the minimum threshold. This typically indicates a short to ground in the sensor circuit, a failed sensor with low output, or a wiring issue pulling the signal voltage down. The PCM expects the sensor signal to remain within a specific voltage window during all IMRC operating conditions.
safetyImpact: Low safety impact. The IMRC system will be disabled by the PCM and the engine will operate with reduced optimization.

causes.cause: Short to ground in the position sensor signal wire
Failed IMRC position sensor with low output
Corroded or water-damaged sensor connector
Damaged sensor ground circuit causing voltage offset

symptoms: Slight performance reduction across RPM range
IMRC system defaults to fixed position

fixSteps.title: Verify Low Signal Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a scan tool and verify the IMRC position sensor voltage is below the normal range (typically below 0.3V when it should vary between 0.5V and 4.5V depending on flap position).

fixSteps.title: Disconnect and Check Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor connector and check if the signal voltage at the PCM rises to reference voltage (approximately 5V). If it does, the sensor or its ground path is pulling the signal low.

fixSteps.title: Check for Short to Ground
fixSteps.instruction: With the sensor disconnected, check the signal wire for continuity to ground. Any reading less than infinite resistance indicates a short to ground in the harness that must be located and repaired.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty sensor if the internal resistance is out of specification. Repair any shorts to ground in the wiring harness. Verify the signal voltage returns to the normal operating range with the key on, engine off and during a test drive.

faq.q: Can water intrusion cause P2016?
faq.a: Yes, water entering the sensor connector can create a low-resistance path to ground, pulling the signal voltage below the minimum threshold. This is especially common in engine bays exposed to heavy rain or pressure washing.

faq.q: Will P2016 cause my car to stall?
faq.a: No, P2016 will not cause stalling. The PCM will disable the IMRC system and the engine will run normally but without the runner length optimization feature.

faq.q: Is this the same as a throttle position sensor code?
faq.a: No, the IMRC position sensor is a completely separate sensor from the throttle position sensor. The IMRC sensor monitors the intake runner flap position, not the throttle blade angle.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner position sensor signal voltage on bank 1 is higher than the maximum expected value.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the signal voltage from the intake manifold runner position sensor on bank 1 is above the maximum threshold. This typically indicates an open circuit in the sensor ground, a short to the reference voltage supply, or a failed sensor with excessively high output. The PCM interprets this as an invalid reading and disables IMRC control.
safetyImpact: Low safety impact. The IMRC system will be disabled and the engine will operate with slightly reduced efficiency.

causes.cause: Open ground circuit to the IMRC position sensor
Short to the 5V reference voltage in the signal wire
Failed position sensor with high output
Disconnected sensor connector (signal floats to reference voltage)

fixSteps.title: Check Connector and Verify High Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the sensor connector is fully seated. Use a scan tool to confirm the sensor voltage is near or above the 5V reference level, which is above the normal operating range.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor Ground Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: With the sensor connected, backprobe the ground wire and measure voltage drop between the sensor ground pin and battery negative. More than 0.1V indicates a poor ground that could cause a high signal reading.

fixSteps.title: Check for Short to Reference Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor and measure continuity between the signal wire and the 5V reference wire. Any continuity indicates the wires are shorted together in the harness.

fixSteps.title: Repair Ground or Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the ground connection if faulty, or repair any wire-to-wire shorts in the harness. If the sensor itself is producing a high output, replace it. Clear codes and verify normal signal voltage range during operation.

faq.q: What causes a high signal voltage on this sensor?
faq.a: The most common cause is a broken or corroded ground wire. When the sensor loses its ground reference, the signal floats up toward the 5V reference voltage, triggering this code.

faq.q: Is P2017 different from P2016?
faq.a: Yes, P2016 indicates the signal voltage is too low (possible short to ground), while P2017 indicates the signal voltage is too high (possible open ground or short to reference voltage). They indicate opposite electrical faults.

faq.q: Can I test the sensor with a simple multimeter?
faq.a: Yes, you can measure the sensor resistance and check for opens or shorts. However, confirming the voltage readings under load is best done with a scan tool that shows live sensor data.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Control Position Sensor A Stuck Closed (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner control position sensor A on bank 1 continuously indicates the flaps are in the closed position regardless of PCM commands.
fullDescription: The PCM has commanded the intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps to open, but the position sensor A continues to report a closed position beyond the expected transition time. This may indicate a genuine mechanical fault where the flaps are physically stuck, or a faulty sensor that is locked at the closed-position value. This code differs from P2006 in that it specifically references the position sensor feedback rather than the actuator status.
safetyImpact: Reduced high-RPM engine output may affect highway driving performance and merging capability.

causes.cause: IMRC flaps physically stuck in the closed position due to carbon
Position sensor A failed in a low-voltage state
IMRC actuator not generating enough force to open flaps
Broken mechanical linkage between actuator and flaps

symptoms: Reduced power output above mid-RPM range
Possible intake noise changes at different RPMs

fixSteps.title: Scan and Review Data
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a scan tool and read live data for the IMRC position sensor A. Command the IMRC actuator to open and observe whether the sensor reading changes at all.

fixSteps.title: Physical Inspection
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the engine cover and visually inspect the IMRC flaps if accessible. Attempt to manually move them while observing the sensor output on the scan tool to determine if the issue is mechanical or sensor-related.

fixSteps.title: Clean and Free IMRC Mechanism
fixSteps.instruction: Clean carbon deposits from the flaps and pivot points using intake cleaner. Lubricate the pivot shafts with a suitable high-temperature lubricant. Verify the flaps move freely through full travel.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Actuator
fixSteps.instruction: If the mechanism moves freely but the sensor still reads stuck closed, replace position sensor A. If the actuator cannot move the flaps, replace the actuator assembly. Clear codes and verify operation across the RPM range.

faq.q: What does 'Position Sensor A' mean?
faq.a: Some IMRC systems use two sensors or a dual-track sensor to provide redundant position feedback. Sensor A is the primary position sensor. Your vehicle may also have a sensor B with its own separate codes.

faq.q: Is this code common?
faq.a: P2089 is most commonly seen on European vehicles with variable-length intake manifolds, particularly Volkswagen, Audi, and BMW models with direct injection engines.

faq.q: Can I clean the carbon without removing the intake manifold?
faq.a: In some cases, using a throttle body/intake cleaner sprayed through the throttle body while the engine is running can help dissolve light carbon deposits. However, heavy buildup usually requires manifold removal for thorough cleaning.

title: Intake Manifold Runner Control Position Sensor B Stuck Open (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The intake manifold runner control position sensor B on bank 1 continuously reports the flaps are in the open position.
fullDescription: The PCM has commanded the intake manifold runner control flaps to close, but position sensor B continues to indicate an open position. Sensor B provides secondary or redundant position feedback for the IMRC system on bank 1. This persistent open reading may indicate a physically stuck flap, a sensor locked at the open-position value, or a wiring issue specific to the B sensor circuit.
safetyImpact: Reduced low-RPM torque may affect responsiveness in city driving and low-speed maneuvering.

causes.cause: IMRC flaps physically stuck open due to broken linkage
Position sensor B failed at the open-position reading
Carbon deposits preventing complete flap closure
Wiring fault specific to sensor B circuit

symptoms: Reduced low-end torque and throttle response
Possible intake air noise at idle

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor B Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read position sensor B live data. Command the IMRC to close and confirm sensor B does not reflect the change. Compare with sensor A reading if available.

fixSteps.title: Inspect IMRC Mechanism
fixSteps.instruction: Visually verify whether the flaps are actually stuck open or if the sensor is providing incorrect feedback. Check the linkage, pivot points, and actuator shaft for damage or disconnection.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor B Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the sensor B connector and measure sensor resistance and circuit continuity. Check for proper reference voltage and ground at the connector. Compare readings to sensor A specifications.

fixSteps.title: Repair or Replace Components
fixSteps.instruction: If the flaps are mechanically stuck, clean carbon deposits or repair broken linkage. If sensor B has failed, replace it. If wiring is damaged, repair the harness. Clear codes and verify both sensor A and B track correctly.

faq.q: What is the difference between Sensor A and Sensor B?
faq.a: In dual-sensor IMRC systems, sensor A and B provide redundant position feedback. They may monitor different flap banks or provide cross-checking data for the PCM to validate flap position accuracy.

faq.q: Can I drive with P2090?
faq.a: Yes, the vehicle is safe to drive. You will notice reduced low-end torque and possibly poorer city fuel economy since the intake runners remain in the high-RPM configuration at all times.

faq.q: Does P2090 always mean the flaps are physically stuck?
faq.a: Not necessarily. The code can also be caused by a failed sensor that reads open even when the flaps have actually closed. Comparing the physical flap position to the sensor reading helps distinguish between mechanical and electrical causes.

title: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too lean as measured downstream of the catalytic converter.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the post-catalyst oxygen sensor (rear O2 sensor) on bank 1 is indicating a persistent lean condition that the short-term and long-term fuel trims cannot fully compensate for. This code differs from standard lean codes (P0171) because it specifically references the post-catalyst measurement, which can indicate issues with the catalyst itself, exhaust leaks between the catalyst and rear O2 sensor, or a faulty rear O2 sensor providing misleading lean readings.
safetyImpact: Lean operation can cause engine overheating and catalyst damage if left unaddressed. Reduced engine performance may affect driving safety.

causes.cause: Exhaust leak between the catalytic converter and rear O2 sensor
Faulty rear (downstream) oxygen sensor reading lean
Deteriorating catalytic converter with reduced efficiency
Vacuum leak or intake air leak on bank 1
Fuel delivery issue (weak fuel pump, clogged injectors)

symptoms: Strong fuel smell from exhaust in some cases

fixSteps.title: Scan and Review Fuel Trim Data
fixSteps.instruction: Read all codes and review long-term and short-term fuel trim data for bank 1. Note whether the front (upstream) O2 sensor also shows lean conditions. If only the rear sensor shows lean, the issue is likely post-catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Exhaust Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the exhaust system between the catalytic converter outlet and the rear O2 sensor for leaks. Check gaskets, flanges, and welded joints. A small exhaust leak here introduces ambient air that makes the rear sensor read lean.

fixSteps.title: Test Rear O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor the rear O2 sensor voltage on the scan tool. A properly functioning post-cat sensor should show a relatively steady voltage near 0.6-0.7V. If it fluctuates rapidly or sits below 0.3V, the sensor may be faulty.

fixSteps.title: Address Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Repair any exhaust leaks found. Replace the rear O2 sensor if it is faulty. If fuel trims on the front sensor also show lean, investigate vacuum leaks or fuel delivery issues. Clear codes and test drive to verify repair.

faq.q: Is P2096 the same as P0171?
faq.a: No. P0171 indicates a system lean condition based on the upstream O2 sensor and fuel trims. P2096 specifically references the post-catalyst measurement, which can indicate issues with the catalyst or rear O2 sensor in addition to general lean conditions.

faq.q: Can a bad catalytic converter cause P2096?
faq.a: Yes, a deteriorating catalytic converter that is not properly processing exhaust gases can affect the rear O2 sensor readings and trigger this code.

faq.q: Will this code cause my car to fail emissions?
faq.a: Yes, a P2096 code will cause an emissions test failure in OBD-II inspection states because it indicates the emissions control system is not operating within specification.

title: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too rich as measured downstream of the catalytic converter.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the post-catalyst oxygen sensor on bank 1 is indicating a persistent rich condition. The downstream O2 sensor voltage remains higher than expected, suggesting the catalytic converter is not adequately oxidizing the excess fuel in the exhaust. This can be caused by an actual rich-running condition that overwhelms the catalyst, a failing catalyst, or a faulty downstream O2 sensor biased toward rich readings.
safetyImpact: Running rich increases catalyst temperature which can lead to catalyst failure and potential fire risk in extreme cases. Reduced engine efficiency affects drivability.

causes.cause: Faulty downstream O2 sensor reading rich
Leaking fuel injector(s) on bank 1
Deteriorating catalytic converter unable to process rich exhaust
Excessive fuel pressure from faulty pressure regulator
Engine coolant temperature sensor reading cold, causing rich fueling

fixSteps.title: Read Codes and Fuel Trim Data
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all codes and review fuel trim data. Check whether the upstream O2 sensor and short-term fuel trims also indicate a rich condition. If only the downstream sensor shows rich, the issue is post-catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Test Downstream O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor the rear O2 sensor on the scan tool during a test drive. The sensor voltage should remain relatively steady between 0.5V and 0.8V. A sensor that stays pegged above 0.9V or responds sluggishly may need replacement.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel System Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a fuel pressure gauge and verify the fuel pressure is within specification at idle and under load. Excessive pressure from a stuck-closed fuel pressure regulator will cause rich operation.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Injectors and Replace Faulty Components
fixSteps.instruction: Perform an injector balance test or leak-down test to identify any leaking injectors. Replace the faulty O2 sensor, fuel pressure regulator, or injectors as needed. Clear codes and verify correction.

faq.q: Can I drive with P2097?
faq.a: You can drive short distances, but a rich condition generates excess heat in the catalytic converter and wastes fuel. Address the issue promptly to prevent catalyst damage.

faq.q: What does post-catalyst mean?
faq.a: Post-catalyst refers to the measurement taken by the downstream O2 sensor, which is located after the catalytic converter in the exhaust system. It monitors how well the catalyst is cleaning the exhaust.

faq.q: Could a clogged air filter cause P2097?
faq.a: A severely clogged air filter can restrict airflow and contribute to a rich condition, but it would typically also affect upstream fuel trims and trigger codes like P0172. It is worth checking but is unlikely to be the sole cause of P2097.

title: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean (Bank 2)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 2 is running too lean as measured downstream of the catalytic converter.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a persistent lean condition on bank 2 as measured by the post-catalyst oxygen sensor. This is the bank 2 equivalent of P2096. On V-type engines, bank 2 is typically the side that does not contain cylinder 1. The lean reading downstream of the catalyst may indicate exhaust leaks, a faulty rear O2 sensor, catalyst deterioration, or an actual lean condition on bank 2.
safetyImpact: Lean combustion can cause elevated exhaust temperatures and potential catalyst damage. Reduced performance may affect driving safety.

causes.cause: Exhaust leak between bank 2 catalytic converter and rear O2 sensor
Faulty bank 2 rear oxygen sensor biased lean
Vacuum leak affecting primarily bank 2 cylinders
Clogged or restricted fuel injector on bank 2

symptoms: Slight hesitation or stumble during acceleration

fixSteps.title: Read Codes and Compare Bank Data
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all codes and compare fuel trim data between bank 1 and bank 2. If only bank 2 shows lean, the problem is isolated to that side. Note if P2096 is also present, indicating a system-wide lean condition.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Bank 2 Exhaust System
fixSteps.instruction: Check the exhaust system on bank 2 for leaks at the exhaust manifold gasket, catalytic converter flanges, and O2 sensor bung. A propane enrichment test near suspected leak areas can help identify leaks.

fixSteps.title: Test Bank 2 Rear O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor the bank 2 rear O2 sensor on the scan tool. Compare its behavior to the bank 1 rear sensor. A properly functioning sensor should show a steady voltage near 0.6-0.7V with minimal fluctuation.

fixSteps.title: Repair Exhaust Leaks or Replace Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Seal or repair any exhaust leaks found on bank 2. Replace the rear O2 sensor if it is not responding correctly. If vacuum leaks are found, replace the affected hoses or gaskets. Clear codes and verify with a test drive.

faq.q: What is bank 2?
faq.a: On V-type engines (V6, V8), bank 2 is the cylinder bank that does not contain cylinder number 1. Consult your vehicle's firing order diagram to identify which side is bank 2.

faq.q: If I have both P2096 and P2098, what does that mean?
faq.a: Having both codes means both banks are running lean post-catalyst, which suggests a system-wide issue such as low fuel pressure, a clogged fuel filter, or a large vacuum leak affecting the entire engine.

faq.q: Can a catalytic converter cause a lean code?
faq.a: A deteriorating catalytic converter can affect the rear O2 sensor readings in ways that appear as a lean or rich condition, even if the actual fuel mixture is correct.

title: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich (Bank 2)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 2 is running too rich as measured downstream of the catalytic converter.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a persistent rich condition on bank 2 based on post-catalyst oxygen sensor readings. This is the bank 2 counterpart to P2097. The downstream O2 sensor on bank 2 is indicating higher-than-expected voltage, suggesting that the catalytic converter is not adequately oxidizing excess fuel, the sensor is faulty, or there is an actual rich condition affecting bank 2 cylinders.
safetyImpact: Excessive richness increases catalyst operating temperatures and may cause overheating. Prolonged operation risks catalyst failure.

causes.cause: Faulty bank 2 downstream O2 sensor biased rich
Leaking fuel injector on bank 2 cylinder
Deteriorating bank 2 catalytic converter
High fuel pressure affecting bank 2 fuel rail

symptoms: Strong fuel odor from the tailpipe
Possible spark plug fouling on bank 2 cylinders

fixSteps.title: Diagnostic Scan and Bank Comparison
fixSteps.instruction: Read all stored codes and compare fuel trim data between bank 1 and bank 2. If only bank 2 shows rich, focus diagnosis on that bank. Check for concurrent codes like P2097 or P0172.

fixSteps.title: Evaluate Downstream O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor the bank 2 downstream O2 sensor voltage. A healthy sensor should read 0.5-0.8V steadily after warm-up. If it stays above 0.9V or is slow to respond to changes, it may be faulty.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Injectors on Bank 2
fixSteps.instruction: Perform an injector balance test to check for leaking or stuck-open injectors on bank 2. Also inspect spark plugs on bank 2 cylinders for soot deposits indicating rich operation.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the downstream O2 sensor if faulty. Replace any leaking injectors. If the catalytic converter is deteriorating, it may need replacement. Clear codes and confirm the fix with a test drive.

faq.q: Why is only bank 2 rich and not bank 1?
faq.a: Bank-specific rich codes usually indicate a localized issue such as a leaking injector on a bank 2 cylinder, a faulty bank 2 downstream O2 sensor, or a deteriorating catalytic converter on the bank 2 exhaust path.

faq.q: Can a bad catalytic converter make the O2 sensor read rich?
faq.a: Yes, a catalyst that has lost its ability to properly oxidize unburned fuel will allow more hydrocarbons to pass through, causing the downstream O2 sensor to read richer than normal.

faq.q: Will replacing just the O2 sensor fix this?
faq.a: If the sensor is the root cause, yes. However, if the sensor is accurately reporting a rich condition caused by a leaking injector or failing catalyst, replacing only the sensor will not fix the underlying problem.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Open
shortDescription: An open circuit has been detected in the electronic throttle body actuator motor wiring.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit in the electronic throttle control (ETC) motor. The ETC motor physically moves the throttle blade based on PCM commands in drive-by-wire systems. An open circuit means the PCM cannot control throttle position, which is a critical safety concern. The vehicle will likely enter limp mode with severely limited throttle response or may not start at all.
safetyImpact: Loss of throttle control is a serious safety issue. The vehicle will enter fail-safe mode with severely restricted power or may be undrivable.

causes.cause: Disconnected throttle body electrical connector
Broken wire in the throttle actuator motor circuit
Failed electronic throttle body motor (open coil winding)
Corroded connector terminals at the throttle body
PCM throttle control driver circuit failure

symptoms: Check Engine Light illuminated with reduced power warning
Vehicle enters limp mode or fail-safe mode
Severely limited throttle response (may not exceed 1500-2000 RPM)
Engine may not start or may stall immediately
Electronic throttle control warning light illuminated

fixSteps.title: Check Throttle Body Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the electronic throttle body on the intake manifold. Verify the electrical connector is fully seated and locked. Disconnect and inspect for bent pins, corrosion, or moisture intrusion.

fixSteps.title: Test Motor Circuit Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: With the connector disconnected, measure the resistance across the throttle motor terminals on the throttle body. Compare to manufacturer specifications (typically 1-6 ohms). An infinite reading confirms an open motor coil.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring Harness Continuity
fixSteps.instruction: Using the wiring diagram, check continuity on each wire from the throttle body connector to the PCM. Flex and wiggle the harness while testing to identify intermittent open circuits caused by chafing or stress fractures.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the motor is open-circuit, replace the entire electronic throttle body assembly. If wiring is the issue, repair using proper splicing techniques and weather-tight connectors. After replacement, the throttle body may require a relearn procedure using the scan tool.

faq.q: Is it safe to drive with P2100?
faq.a: No, this code indicates a critical throttle control failure. The vehicle should not be driven and should be towed to a repair facility. Even in limp mode, the lack of throttle control makes the vehicle unsafe.

faq.q: Do I need to reprogram the PCM after replacing the throttle body?
faq.a: Usually no, but the PCM may need a throttle body relearn procedure, which can typically be performed with a scan tool or by following a specific key-on/off sequence described in the service manual.

faq.q: Can a loose gas cap cause P2100?
faq.a: No, a loose gas cap is related to the EVAP system and does not affect the electronic throttle control circuit. P2100 is strictly an electrical fault in the throttle motor circuit.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The electronic throttle actuator motor is not achieving the expected throttle position within the specified time or range.
fullDescription: The PCM has determined that the electronic throttle control motor is connected and responding, but its performance is outside acceptable parameters. The throttle blade position does not match the commanded position within the allowed tolerance or response time. This can indicate a binding throttle blade, weak motor, or intermittent electrical connection that degrades motor performance without causing a complete open or short circuit.
safetyImpact: Unpredictable throttle response is a serious safety concern. The vehicle may surge or have delayed throttle response in traffic.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup on throttle blade causing binding
Worn or weak electronic throttle body motor
High resistance in the throttle motor circuit wiring
Faulty throttle position sensor providing incorrect feedback
Throttle body internal gear mechanism wear

symptoms: Check Engine Light with electronic throttle warning
Engine RPM fluctuations or hunting at idle

fixSteps.title: Read Codes and Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all codes and monitor throttle position sensor commanded vs actual positions on the scan tool. Note the difference between commanded and actual throttle angle at various pedal positions.

fixSteps.title: Clean Electronic Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct from the throttle body and carefully clean the throttle blade and bore using throttle body cleaner and a soft cloth. Do not force the blade open on electronic throttle bodies; instead, use the scan tool to command it open.

fixSteps.title: Check Motor Circuit Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the motor winding resistance at the throttle body connector and compare to specifications. Also check the wiring for high-resistance connections by measuring voltage drop across each wire while the motor is being commanded.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body and Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning does not resolve the issue and the motor or internal gears are worn, replace the electronic throttle body. Perform the throttle body relearn procedure per the manufacturer instructions. Clear codes and test drive to verify smooth throttle response.

faq.q: Can cleaning the throttle body fix P2101?
faq.a: Yes, in many cases, carbon buildup on the throttle blade is the root cause. A thorough cleaning followed by a throttle body relearn procedure often resolves this code.

faq.q: Why does my car go into limp mode with this code?
faq.a: When the PCM detects that the throttle is not responding as expected, it enters limp mode as a safety measure to prevent uncontrolled acceleration. This limits engine power to protect the driver.

faq.q: Is P2101 related to the accelerator pedal?
faq.a: Not directly. P2101 relates to the throttle body motor, not the accelerator pedal position sensor. However, the PCM compares pedal input to throttle response, so a pedal sensor issue could contribute to the condition.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Low
shortDescription: The electronic throttle actuator motor control circuit voltage is below the expected minimum level.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the voltage on the electronic throttle control motor circuit is lower than the minimum expected threshold. This indicates a possible short to ground in the motor winding or control wiring, excessive current draw from a binding motor, or a low-side driver issue within the PCM. The low voltage prevents the PCM from properly controlling throttle position.
safetyImpact: Critical safety concern due to potential loss of throttle control. Vehicle will likely enter fail-safe mode.

causes.cause: Short to ground in throttle motor wiring harness
Internally shorted throttle body motor windings
Damaged or pinched wiring near the throttle body
Water or moisture intrusion into the throttle body connector

symptoms: Check Engine Light with throttle control warning
Very limited or no throttle response
Engine may stall or refuse to start
Blown fuse in throttle control circuit

fixSteps.title: Check Fuse and Basic Power
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and test the throttle control circuit fuse. If blown, check for a short before replacing. Verify battery voltage at the throttle body connector with the key on.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Wiring for Short to Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the throttle body connector. Using a multimeter, check each motor control wire for continuity to ground. Any reading below infinite resistance indicates a short to ground in the harness.

fixSteps.title: Test Throttle Body Motor
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the resistance of the motor windings at the throttle body. Compare to specifications. Significantly lower than specified resistance indicates shorted motor windings.

fixSteps.title: Repair Short or Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Locate and repair the short to ground in the wiring harness. If the throttle body motor is internally shorted, replace the throttle body assembly. Perform the relearn procedure, clear codes, and test drive.

faq.q: Why does my throttle fuse keep blowing?
faq.a: A repeatedly blown fuse indicates a short to ground in the throttle motor circuit. The short must be located and repaired before the system can function properly. Do not install a higher-rated fuse as this can damage the PCM.

faq.q: Can I bypass the electronic throttle body?
faq.a: No, the electronic throttle body is a critical safety component in drive-by-wire systems and cannot be safely bypassed. The PCM relies on it for proper engine operation and safety features like traction control.

faq.q: Could moisture cause this code?
faq.a: Yes, moisture entering the throttle body connector or motor can create a low-resistance path to ground, causing the circuit voltage to drop below threshold. Dry and seal the connector thoroughly.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit High
shortDescription: The electronic throttle actuator motor control circuit voltage is above the expected maximum level.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the voltage on the electronic throttle control motor circuit is higher than the maximum expected threshold. This typically indicates an open ground circuit, a short to battery voltage in the motor wiring, or a high-side driver fault within the PCM. This condition prevents proper current flow through the motor and renders throttle control inoperative.
safetyImpact: Critical safety issue. Loss of throttle control causes the vehicle to enter fail-safe mode. Do not drive.

causes.cause: Open ground wire in the throttle motor circuit
Short to battery voltage in a motor control wire
Corroded ground connection at the throttle body
PCM high-side driver failure

symptoms: Check Engine Light with throttle control warning

fixSteps.title: Check Ground Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the throttle body ground wire and its connection point. Clean any corrosion from the ground terminal and ensure a solid metal-to-metal contact. Check for voltage drop on the ground circuit (should be less than 0.1V).

fixSteps.title: Test for Short to Power
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the throttle body connector and the PCM connector. Check each motor circuit wire for continuity to battery positive. Any reading indicates a short to power source in the harness.

fixSteps.title: Measure Motor Circuit Voltages
fixSteps.instruction: With the throttle body connected and key on, measure the voltage at each motor terminal relative to battery ground. Compare to expected values in the service manual to identify which wire has the abnormal high voltage.

fixSteps.title: Repair Wiring or Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the ground connection, fix any shorts to power, or replace the throttle body if the internal circuit is at fault. Perform a throttle relearn, clear codes, and verify proper operation.

faq.q: Is P2103 more serious than P2102?
faq.a: Both are equally serious as they prevent proper throttle control. P2102 indicates low voltage (short to ground) while P2103 indicates high voltage (open ground or short to power). Both cause fail-safe mode.

faq.q: Can a bad ground really cause all this?
faq.a: Yes, the throttle motor requires a solid ground to function. A corroded or broken ground connection is one of the most common causes of P2103 and is often the easiest to fix.

faq.q: Should I tow the car or can I drive it?
faq.a: The vehicle should be towed. While limp mode may allow very limited driving, the underlying throttle control failure makes the vehicle unsafe for road use.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Forced Idle
shortDescription: The PCM has forced the engine to idle due to a detected fault in the electronic throttle control system.
fullDescription: The PCM has activated the forced idle strategy as a fail-safe response to a detected malfunction in the electronic throttle control system. When the PCM cannot trust the throttle position or detects conflicting signals from throttle-related sensors, it locks the throttle at or near idle to prevent unintended acceleration. This code is typically accompanied by other throttle-related codes that identify the specific fault triggering the forced idle condition.
safetyImpact: Vehicle is limited to idle speed. While this is a safety measure, the vehicle is essentially undrivable and should not be used in traffic.

causes.cause: Conflicting throttle position sensor signals (TPS1 vs TPS2)
Accelerator pedal position sensor disagreement
Throttle actuator motor fault triggering safety shutdown
PCM detecting stuck throttle condition

symptoms: Check Engine Light illuminated with multiple warnings
Engine locked at idle speed, will not accelerate
Reduced power mode indicator on dashboard
Accelerator pedal input has no effect on engine RPM

fixSteps.title: Read All Stored Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all stored and pending diagnostic trouble codes. P2104 is a consequence code, so other codes present will identify the root cause. Focus on throttle position sensor codes, accelerator pedal codes, and throttle motor codes.

fixSteps.title: Monitor Throttle System Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: View live data for TPS1, TPS2, APP1, APP2 (accelerator pedal position), and commanded vs actual throttle angle. Identify which sensor or actuator is providing abnormal readings.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Body and Pedal Assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Visually inspect the electronic throttle body connector and accelerator pedal assembly connector for damage, corrosion, or loose connections. Check the throttle body for carbon buildup or mechanical binding.

fixSteps.title: Repair Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Address the root cause identified by the accompanying codes. This may involve replacing the throttle body, accelerator pedal position sensor assembly, or repairing wiring. After repair, clear all codes and perform the throttle body relearn procedure.

faq.q: Why is P2104 always accompanied by other codes?
faq.a: P2104 is a response code, not a cause code. It indicates the PCM has activated the forced idle safety strategy because of another fault. The accompanying codes reveal what triggered the safety response.

faq.q: Can I clear P2104 and drive normally?
faq.a: You can clear it, but if the underlying fault is still present, the code will return immediately and the vehicle will re-enter forced idle. The root cause must be repaired first.

faq.q: Is forced idle the same as limp mode?
faq.a: Forced idle is more restrictive than typical limp mode. Limp mode usually allows limited acceleration up to about 2000-3000 RPM, while forced idle locks the engine at idle speed with no acceleration capability.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Forced Engine Shutdown
shortDescription: The PCM has commanded a forced engine shutdown due to a critical electronic throttle control system failure.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a critical failure in the electronic throttle control system that warrants an immediate engine shutdown as the highest-level safety response. This code is set when the PCM determines that neither forced idle nor limp mode can safely manage the detected fault. This is the most severe throttle-related safety action and indicates a condition where continued engine operation could result in uncontrolled acceleration or other dangerous situations.
safetyImpact: Critical safety code. Engine shutdown is an emergency response to prevent potential uncontrolled acceleration.

causes.cause: Simultaneous failure of redundant throttle position sensors
Throttle stuck open and not responding to close commands
Critical PCM internal throttle control circuit failure
Complete loss of communication with the throttle actuator

symptoms: Engine shuts off unexpectedly while driving
Check Engine Light and multiple dashboard warnings illuminated
Engine will not restart or restarts and immediately shuts off
Possible check engine, traction control, and ABS lights all illuminated

fixSteps.title: Safely Secure the Vehicle
fixSteps.instruction: If the engine shut down while driving, guide the vehicle to a safe location. Turn on hazard lights. Do not attempt to restart repeatedly. The vehicle should be towed to a repair facility.

fixSteps.title: Complete Diagnostic Scan
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a full system scan reading all modules for stored codes. Document all throttle-related codes as they will identify the chain of events leading to the forced shutdown.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Body and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Thoroughly inspect the electronic throttle body, its connector, and all wiring between the throttle body, accelerator pedal, and PCM. Check for physical damage, rodent chewing, or heat-related insulation failure.

fixSteps.title: Replace Failed Components
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the throttle body assembly if internal faults are found. Replace the accelerator pedal position sensor if both sensor tracks have failed. In rare cases, the PCM may need replacement. Perform full relearn procedure after repair.

faq.q: Is a forced engine shutdown dangerous?
faq.a: The shutdown itself is a safety measure. However, losing engine power while driving means losing power steering assist and brake boost after a few applications. Guide the vehicle to a stop as safely as possible.

faq.q: Why did my car shut off while driving?
faq.a: P2105 indicates the PCM detected a critical throttle control failure where the safest action was to stop the engine. This prevents potential uncontrolled acceleration.

faq.q: Is this a recall-related issue?
faq.a: Some vehicle manufacturers have issued recalls related to electronic throttle control failures. Check with your dealer or the NHTSA recalls database using your VIN to determine if a recall applies to your vehicle.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Forced Limited Power
shortDescription: The PCM has forced the engine into a limited power mode due to a fault in the electronic throttle control system.
fullDescription: The PCM has activated a forced limited power strategy as a safety response to a detected throttle control system malfunction. Unlike forced idle (P2104), this mode allows limited acceleration but caps the maximum throttle opening to a safe level, typically allowing speeds up to 30-45 mph. This intermediate safety response is used when the PCM determines the throttle can be partially controlled but full control is not possible.
safetyImpact: Vehicle power is significantly limited. While safer than full failure, highway driving is not safe in this mode due to inability to maintain adequate speed.

causes.cause: Intermittent throttle position sensor signal fault
Partially degraded accelerator pedal position sensor
High resistance in throttle motor circuit limiting current
Throttle body not reaching full range due to partial binding

symptoms: Check Engine Light and reduced power warning
Maximum speed limited to approximately 30-45 mph
Dashboard displays limp mode or wrench icon

fixSteps.title: Scan for Root Cause Codes
fixSteps.instruction: P2106 is a consequence code. Read all stored codes to find the primary fault. Look for throttle position sensor, accelerator pedal, or throttle motor codes that triggered the limited power mode.

fixSteps.title: Check Throttle System Connections
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all connectors in the throttle control system: throttle body connector, accelerator pedal connector, and any intermediate harness connectors. Look for loose fit, corrosion, or damaged terminals.

fixSteps.title: Monitor Sensor Signals
fixSteps.instruction: Use the scan tool to graph TPS1, TPS2, APP1, and APP2 signals while slowly pressing and releasing the accelerator. Look for signal dropouts, dead spots, or inconsistencies between redundant signals.

fixSteps.title: Repair Identified Fault
fixSteps.instruction: Based on the root cause, replace the throttle body, accelerator pedal sensor, or repair wiring. Perform throttle relearn procedure. Clear all codes and test drive under various conditions to confirm full power is restored.

faq.q: Can I still drive in limited power mode?
faq.a: Only for short distances at low speeds to reach a safe location or repair shop. The limited power mode is not intended for normal driving and may not allow safe highway operation.

faq.q: Will turning the car off and on fix it?
faq.a: Sometimes cycling the ignition can temporarily clear the limited power mode if the fault is intermittent. However, the underlying issue must still be diagnosed and repaired as the condition will recur.

faq.q: Is P2106 covered under powertrain warranty?
faq.a: The electronic throttle control system is typically covered under the powertrain warranty. Check with your dealer as some manufacturers have extended coverage for known throttle-related issues.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Module Processor
shortDescription: An internal processor error has been detected in the throttle actuator control module.
fullDescription: The PCM or a dedicated throttle control module has detected an internal processor fault related to the electronic throttle control system. This may indicate a hardware failure within the control module's throttle processing circuits, corrupted software, or a communication error between the throttle control processor and the main PCM processor. This is a rare code that typically points to a control module issue rather than a sensor or actuator problem.
safetyImpact: A processor fault in the throttle control system is a critical safety concern. The vehicle will enter fail-safe mode immediately.

causes.cause: Internal PCM or throttle module processor malfunction
Corrupted PCM software or calibration data
Electrical power supply issue causing processor instability
Overheating of the control module causing intermittent faults

symptoms: Check Engine Light with multiple system warnings
Vehicle enters fail-safe or limp mode
Intermittent or complete loss of throttle response
Multiple unrelated codes may be stored simultaneously
Vehicle may stall or refuse to start

fixSteps.title: Verify Power and Ground to PCM
fixSteps.instruction: Check the PCM power supply and ground circuits for proper voltage and low resistance. Ensure battery voltage is above 12.4V and that all PCM ground straps are clean and tight.

fixSteps.title: Check for TSBs and Software Updates
fixSteps.instruction: Research manufacturer technical service bulletins (TSBs) for your specific vehicle. Many P2107 issues are resolved through PCM software updates or reprogramming rather than hardware replacement.

fixSteps.title: Inspect PCM and Connectors
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the PCM connector(s) and inspect all terminals for corrosion, bent pins, or water intrusion. Check the PCM housing for signs of overheating, physical damage, or water entry.

fixSteps.title: Reprogram or Replace PCM
fixSteps.instruction: If a TSB exists, have the PCM reprogrammed with updated software. If the hardware has failed, the PCM must be replaced and programmed to the vehicle. This typically requires dealer-level diagnostic tools.

faq.q: Can I fix a P2107 myself?
faq.a: Diagnosing the power supply and grounds is possible for a DIYer, but PCM reprogramming or replacement requires specialized equipment and software that is typically only available at dealerships or well-equipped independent shops.

faq.q: Does a P2107 always mean the PCM is bad?
faq.a: Not always. Sometimes a software update resolves the issue, or a poor electrical connection to the PCM causes intermittent processor faults. Hardware failure is confirmed only after ruling out power supply and software issues.

faq.q: How much does a PCM replacement cost?
faq.a: PCM replacement costs vary widely by vehicle, typically ranging from $500 to $1500 including parts and programming. Used or remanufactured PCMs can reduce costs but must be properly programmed to your vehicle.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Module Performance
shortDescription: The throttle actuator control module is not performing within expected parameters.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the throttle actuator control module is not processing throttle commands or managing throttle position within the expected performance envelope. This code indicates a broader performance issue with the throttle control strategy rather than a specific circuit fault. It may be set when the module's response time, accuracy, or internal diagnostics fall outside acceptable limits.
safetyImpact: Throttle control performance degradation is a serious safety concern that may result in unpredictable throttle behavior.

causes.cause: Degraded PCM throttle control processor performance
Throttle body requiring relearn after battery disconnect
Intermittent electrical issues affecting module performance
Aftermarket throttle body or parts incompatibility

symptoms: Intermittent limp mode or reduced power episodes
Occasional stalling, especially when coming to a stop

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Relearn Procedure
fixSteps.instruction: Before extensive diagnosis, perform the throttle body relearn procedure using the scan tool or the manufacturer-specific key cycle method. This recalibrates the PCM's throttle control parameters and often resolves P2108.

fixSteps.title: Check Battery and Charging System
fixSteps.instruction: Test the battery state of charge and the alternator output. Low or fluctuating voltage can cause module performance issues. Verify battery voltage is above 12.4V with the engine off and 13.5-14.5V while running.

fixSteps.title: Review for TSBs and Updates
fixSteps.instruction: Check for manufacturer technical service bulletins related to throttle control performance issues for your specific vehicle year, make, and model. Many P2108 codes are addressed through calibration updates.

fixSteps.title: Replace Module or Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If relearn and software updates do not resolve the issue, the throttle body or PCM may need replacement. Verify that any replacement parts are OEM or OEM-equivalent to ensure compatibility with the control strategy.

faq.q: Can a dead battery cause P2108?
faq.a: Yes, a battery disconnect or a severely drained battery can cause the PCM to lose its learned throttle body parameters, triggering P2108 until a relearn procedure is completed.

faq.q: I installed an aftermarket throttle body and got P2108. Why?
faq.a: Aftermarket throttle bodies may have slightly different motor characteristics or sensor calibrations that the PCM cannot adapt to. Using an OEM or OEM-spec throttle body is recommended.

faq.q: Will a simple code clear fix P2108?
faq.a: Sometimes clearing the code and performing a throttle relearn is all that is needed, especially after a battery disconnect. If the code returns after the relearn, further diagnosis is needed.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Forced Limited RPM
shortDescription: The PCM has limited engine RPM as a safety measure due to a throttle actuator control system malfunction.
fullDescription: The PCM has activated an RPM limiting strategy in response to a detected throttle control system fault. This limits the maximum engine speed to a predetermined safe level, typically 2000-3000 RPM, preventing the engine from reaching higher speeds where a throttle malfunction could be more dangerous. Like P2104 and P2106, this is a consequence code and will be accompanied by codes identifying the root cause.
safetyImpact: Vehicle is speed-limited and may not be able to maintain highway speeds. Not safe for highway or high-speed driving.

causes.cause: Throttle position sensor correlation error between tracks
Accelerator pedal position sensor disagreement
Throttle motor performance out of specification
Communication fault between throttle module and main PCM

symptoms: Check Engine Light with reduced power warning
Engine RPM capped at 2000-3000 RPM regardless of pedal input
Vehicle unable to reach normal highway speeds

fixSteps.title: Identify Root Cause Codes
fixSteps.instruction: Scan all modules for stored codes. P2110 indicates the symptom (RPM limiting) but not the cause. Identify the primary fault code that triggered the RPM limit strategy.

fixSteps.title: Check Throttle and Pedal Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor TPS1/TPS2 correlation and APP1/APP2 correlation on the scan tool. The PCM expects these redundant sensor pairs to track within a specific percentage of each other. Identify which sensor pair is out of correlation.

fixSteps.title: Inspect All Throttle System Components
fixSteps.instruction: Check the throttle body connector, accelerator pedal connector, and all related wiring for damage. Clean the throttle body if carbon buildup is affecting blade movement. Verify no aftermarket accessories are interfering with the throttle system.

fixSteps.title: Repair and Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Replace any faulty sensors, repair wiring, or replace the throttle body as indicated by the root cause. Perform throttle body relearn and accelerator pedal relearn procedures. Clear all codes and test drive to confirm full RPM range is restored.

faq.q: What is forced limited RPM?
faq.a: It is a safety strategy where the PCM caps the maximum engine speed to prevent potential runaway acceleration caused by a throttle control malfunction. It allows the vehicle to be driven at low speeds to a safe location.

faq.q: Can I override the RPM limit?
faq.a: No, and attempting to bypass the PCM safety strategy is extremely dangerous. The RPM limit exists because the PCM has detected a condition that makes full throttle control unreliable. Fix the underlying problem instead.

faq.q: Will disconnecting the battery reset the RPM limit?
faq.a: Disconnecting the battery may temporarily clear the codes, but if the fault is still present, the codes will return and the RPM limit will be reactivated immediately.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Stuck Open
shortDescription: The electronic throttle blade is stuck in an open position and will not close when commanded.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the electronic throttle blade is stuck in an open or partially open position and cannot return to the closed or idle position when commanded. This is a critical safety condition because a stuck-open throttle means the engine may continue to produce power even when the driver lifts off the accelerator pedal. The PCM will typically respond by cutting fuel injection or shutting down the engine.
safetyImpact: Critical safety hazard. A stuck-open throttle can cause unintended acceleration. The PCM will attempt emergency shutdown procedures.

causes.cause: Foreign object lodged in the throttle bore preventing blade closure
Severe carbon buildup preventing throttle blade from closing
Failed throttle return spring inside the throttle body
Throttle body internal gear mechanism jammed
Ice formation on the throttle blade in cold weather

symptoms: Engine RPM remains elevated when pedal is released
Check Engine Light with immediate limp mode or engine shutdown
Vehicle accelerates without driver pedal input
High idle speed that does not decrease
PCM may cut fuel injection as emergency measure

fixSteps.title: Emergency Response
fixSteps.instruction: If the throttle is stuck open while driving, shift to neutral, apply brakes firmly, and steer to a safe location. Turn off the engine. Do not attempt to drive the vehicle further.

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection of Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: With the engine off and key removed, remove the intake duct from the throttle body. Visually inspect the throttle blade and bore for foreign objects, carbon buildup, or mechanical damage. Try to manually move the blade.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If carbon is the cause, thoroughly clean the throttle blade, bore, and blade edges using throttle body cleaner. Pay special attention to the area where the blade seats when closed. Ensure the blade moves freely through its full range.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body if Necessary
fixSteps.instruction: If the internal return spring, gears, or motor are damaged, replace the throttle body assembly. After installation, perform the throttle body relearn procedure. Clear codes and carefully test drive, starting in an empty parking lot.

faq.q: What should I do if my throttle sticks open while driving?
faq.a: Immediately shift to neutral to disconnect the engine from the wheels, apply firm brake pressure, and steer to a safe location. Turn off the engine and do not attempt to restart it. Call for a tow.

faq.q: Can a stuck throttle cause a runaway car?
faq.a: Modern vehicles have multiple safety systems including the PCM cutting fuel injection. However, a stuck throttle before the PCM can respond is dangerous. Always know how to shift to neutral in an emergency.

faq.q: Is this related to the Toyota unintended acceleration recalls?
faq.a: While similar in concept, P2111 is a generic OBD-II code that can appear on any vehicle with electronic throttle control. The Toyota issue involved specific mechanical and electronic concerns unique to those vehicles.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Stuck Closed
shortDescription: The electronic throttle blade is stuck in the closed position and will not open when commanded.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the electronic throttle blade remains in the closed or near-closed position despite commands to open it. This means the engine cannot increase RPM above idle because the throttle blade will not allow additional airflow. While less immediately dangerous than a stuck-open throttle, a stuck-closed throttle renders the vehicle undrivable and may cause stalling.
safetyImpact: Vehicle cannot accelerate and may stall in traffic. Loss of power in moving traffic presents a safety hazard.

causes.cause: Heavy carbon buildup preventing throttle blade from opening
Failed electronic throttle body motor unable to open blade
Throttle body internal gear mechanism broken
Ice formation preventing throttle blade movement in extreme cold

symptoms: Engine will not accelerate above idle
Check Engine Light with throttle control warnings
Accelerator pedal has no effect on engine RPM
Engine may stall when put in gear

fixSteps.title: Verify Throttle Position
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to compare commanded throttle position with actual throttle position. If the PCM is commanding the throttle open but the actual position remains near 0%, the blade is physically stuck.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct and visually inspect the throttle blade. Check if the blade moves when the accelerator is pressed with the engine off and key on. Listen for the motor attempting to actuate.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the throttle blade and bore thoroughly with throttle body cleaner. Remove all carbon deposits that could prevent the blade from opening. Work the blade gently by hand to verify free movement.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If the motor has failed or the internal gear mechanism is broken, the throttle body must be replaced. Install the new unit, perform the relearn procedure, clear codes, and verify full throttle operation before driving.

faq.q: Is a stuck-closed throttle dangerous?
faq.a: While less immediately dangerous than stuck-open, a stuck-closed throttle can cause stalling in traffic, which is hazardous. The vehicle cannot accelerate and should be towed.

faq.q: Can I force the throttle blade open manually?
faq.a: You should not force the electronic throttle blade open as this can damage the internal gear mechanism. If the blade will not move with gentle pressure, the motor or gears are likely the issue.
