faq.q: Could extreme cold weather cause this?
faq.a: Yes, in extremely cold conditions, condensation can freeze on the throttle blade and lock it closed. Warming the engine bay with a block heater or parking in a heated garage can prevent this.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Current Range/Performance
shortDescription: The current draw of the electronic throttle actuator motor is outside the expected range during operation.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the electric current flowing through the throttle actuator motor is not within the expected range for the commanded throttle position. This can mean the motor is drawing too much current (indicating mechanical binding or a partial short) or too little current (indicating high resistance or a failing motor). The PCM monitors motor current as part of its comprehensive throttle control safety strategy.
safetyImpact: Abnormal motor current indicates degraded throttle control. The vehicle will likely enter a limited power mode.

causes.cause: Throttle body motor bearings worn causing increased friction
Carbon buildup creating mechanical resistance on throttle blade
Partially shorted motor windings increasing current draw
High-resistance connections reducing current flow to motor

symptoms: Check Engine Light with reduced power mode
Throttle blade moves slowly or erratically
Possible buzzing noise from the throttle body

fixSteps.title: Read Codes and Monitor Current
fixSteps.instruction: Using an advanced scan tool, monitor the throttle motor current draw while commanding the throttle through its range. Note any positions where current spikes or drops abnormally.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct and clean the throttle body thoroughly. Carbon deposits increase the mechanical load on the motor, causing higher current draw. Verify the blade moves smoothly after cleaning.

fixSteps.title: Check Motor and Wiring Resistance
fixSteps.instruction: Measure motor winding resistance at the throttle body connector and compare to specifications. Also measure the resistance of the supply and ground wires between the PCM and throttle body.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If the motor windings are out of spec or internal bearings are worn, replace the throttle body assembly. Repair any high-resistance wiring connections found. Perform relearn, clear codes, and test drive.

faq.q: What causes abnormal motor current?
faq.a: The most common cause is carbon buildup making the motor work harder to move the throttle blade. Worn motor bearings, partial motor winding shorts, and high-resistance wiring can also cause abnormal current readings.

faq.q: Can I measure the motor current myself?
faq.a: Measuring motor current typically requires an advanced scan tool that monitors PCM internal data. A clamp-on ammeter on the motor supply wire can also work but requires accessing the wire.

faq.q: Is cleaning the throttle body a good preventive measure?
faq.a: Yes, cleaning the throttle body every 50,000 to 75,000 miles as part of routine maintenance can prevent carbon-related throttle issues and extend the life of the throttle body motor.

title: Throttle Actuator Control Throttle Body Range/Performance
shortDescription: The electronic throttle body is not achieving the expected range of motion or performance during operation.
fullDescription: The PCM has determined that the electronic throttle body is not meeting performance expectations across its operating range. The actual throttle blade position consistently deviates from the commanded position beyond acceptable tolerances during normal driving conditions. This differs from stuck-open/stuck-closed codes in that the throttle is moving but not accurately tracking the PCM commands.
safetyImpact: Inaccurate throttle positioning can cause unpredictable vehicle behavior including surging, hesitation, or unexpected acceleration changes.

causes.cause: Worn throttle body motor or gear mechanism
Carbon deposits limiting throttle blade travel range
Throttle position sensor calibration drift
Weak throttle body return spring

symptoms: Check Engine Light with possible reduced power
Engine surging or hunting at steady throttle
Delayed response when pressing or releasing accelerator
Idle speed that varies from the target

fixSteps.title: Graph Commanded vs Actual Throttle
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to graph commanded throttle position versus actual throttle position simultaneously during a test drive. Note the deviation percentage and which operating conditions produce the worst tracking.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body Thoroughly
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the intake duct and perform a thorough throttle body cleaning, paying special attention to the blade edges and the bore where the blade seats. Carbon on these surfaces directly affects positioning accuracy.

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Body Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: After cleaning, perform the throttle body relearn procedure to recalibrate the PCM to the cleaned throttle body. This adjusts the closed position, open position, and intermediate calibration points.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body if Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If cleaning and relearn do not bring the tracking accuracy within specification, the throttle body has internal wear and must be replaced. Install a new unit, perform relearn, clear codes, and verify accurate tracking.

faq.q: How accurate should the throttle tracking be?
faq.a: The actual throttle position should track within 2-5% of the commanded position during normal operation. Deviations larger than this trigger performance codes like P2119.

faq.q: Can a throttle relearn fix P2119 without cleaning?
faq.a: Sometimes, but if carbon buildup is the root cause, the relearn may temporarily improve tracking only for the code to return as the PCM adapts to the reduced range of motion.

faq.q: Is this code common on high-mileage vehicles?
faq.a: Yes, P2119 becomes more common at higher mileages as the throttle body motor, gears, and return spring wear over time. Regular throttle body cleaning can delay the onset of this issue.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch D Circuit Low
shortDescription: The accelerator pedal position sensor D circuit voltage is below the minimum expected threshold.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the signal voltage from accelerator pedal position (APP) sensor D is below the minimum expected value. In drive-by-wire systems, the accelerator pedal contains multiple position sensors for redundancy. Sensor D (typically APP Sensor 1) provides the primary pedal position signal. A low voltage reading suggests a short to ground, sensor failure, or wiring issue in the sensor D circuit.
safetyImpact: Loss of accurate pedal position data affects throttle control. The vehicle will enter reduced power or limp mode.

causes.cause: Short to ground in the APP sensor D signal wire
Failed accelerator pedal position sensor D
Corroded or water-damaged pedal assembly connector
Damaged wiring in the floor area under the pedal

symptoms: Check Engine Light with reduced power mode
Vehicle enters limp mode with very limited acceleration
Engine may idle higher or lower than normal

fixSteps.title: Verify Low Voltage Reading
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a scan tool and verify that APP Sensor D voltage is below the expected minimum (typically below 0.2V when it should read approximately 0.5-0.8V at rest). Also check APP Sensor E/F for comparison.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Pedal Assembly Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the accelerator pedal position sensor connector, usually near the top of the accelerator pedal assembly in the footwell. Disconnect and inspect for corrosion, bent pins, or moisture. Check for floor mat interference with the pedal.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor D Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: With the connector disconnected, check the signal wire for a short to ground using a multimeter. Also verify that the 5V reference voltage is present at the connector and the ground is solid.

fixSteps.title: Replace Pedal Assembly or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor has failed, the entire accelerator pedal assembly typically must be replaced as the sensors are integrated. If the wiring is damaged, repair it. Clear codes and verify all APP sensor readings are correct at rest and through the full pedal range.

faq.q: Can a floor mat cause P2122?
faq.a: While a floor mat cannot directly cause a low voltage reading, an improperly secured floor mat can interfere with the pedal position sensor by restricting pedal travel. Always ensure floor mats are properly secured.

faq.q: Why does the pedal assembly need to be replaced as a unit?
faq.a: The position sensors are built into the pedal assembly and are not separately replaceable on most vehicles. The entire pedal module must be replaced to restore proper sensor function.

faq.q: Can I drive to a repair shop with P2122?
faq.a: In most cases, the vehicle will be in limp mode with severely limited acceleration. If you can safely reach a shop at reduced speed, it may be possible, but towing is recommended for safety.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch E Circuit Low
shortDescription: The accelerator pedal position sensor E circuit voltage is below the minimum expected threshold.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the signal voltage from accelerator pedal position (APP) sensor E is below the minimum expected value. Sensor E is the secondary pedal position sensor that provides redundant feedback for safety. When both sensors D and E are functional, the PCM cross-references their signals to verify pedal position accuracy. A low voltage on sensor E reduces the system's redundancy and safety margin.
safetyImpact: Reduced sensor redundancy compromises the safety of the drive-by-wire system. Vehicle will enter reduced power mode.

causes.cause: Short to ground in the APP sensor E signal wire
Failed APP sensor E within the pedal assembly
Water intrusion into the pedal assembly connector
Chafed wiring under the dashboard or through the firewall

symptoms: Reduced power mode or limp mode
Accelerator response is delayed or limited

fixSteps.title: Read Sensor E Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor APP Sensor E voltage at rest and through the full pedal range. Compare it with Sensor D readings. Sensor E should track in a complementary pattern to Sensor D.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Connector and Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Check the pedal assembly connector for moisture, corrosion, or damage. Trace the Sensor E wiring for any signs of chafing, especially where the harness passes through the firewall or under the dashboard.

fixSteps.title: Test Sensor E Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the pedal connector and verify the 5V reference and ground at the sensor E pins. Check the signal wire for a short to ground. Measure sensor resistance if specifications are available.

fixSteps.title: Replace Pedal Assembly or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the accelerator pedal assembly if the sensor has failed internally. Repair any wiring faults found. Clear codes and verify both Sensor D and E track correctly through the full pedal range.

faq.q: If Sensor D is still working, why does the car go into limp mode?
faq.a: The drive-by-wire system requires redundant sensor signals for safety. When one sensor fails, the PCM cannot verify pedal position accuracy and limits power as a safety precaution.

faq.q: Can I just replace Sensor E instead of the whole pedal?
faq.a: On most vehicles, the APP sensors are integrated into the pedal assembly and cannot be individually replaced. The entire pedal module must be changed.

faq.q: Is P2127 related to P2122?
faq.a: Both codes relate to the accelerator pedal sensors but address different sensor circuits. P2122 is Sensor D (primary) and P2127 is Sensor E (secondary). Having both codes simultaneously suggests a connector or common ground issue.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch E Circuit High
shortDescription: The accelerator pedal position sensor E circuit voltage is above the maximum expected threshold.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the signal voltage from accelerator pedal position (APP) sensor E exceeds the maximum expected value. This high voltage indicates a potential open ground, short to the reference voltage, or a sensor failure producing an excessively high output. The PCM treats this as a sensor integrity failure and reduces engine power for safety.
safetyImpact: Compromised pedal position sensing reduces the safety of the throttle control system. The vehicle will enter fail-safe mode.

causes.cause: Open ground circuit to APP sensor E
Short between signal wire and 5V reference wire
Failed APP sensor E producing excessive voltage
Disconnected pedal sensor connector allowing signal to float high

symptoms: Check Engine Light with reduced power warning
Vehicle enters limp mode or fail-safe mode
Dashboard displays electronic throttle control warning

fixSteps.title: Verify High Voltage Condition
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to verify that APP Sensor E voltage is above the maximum threshold (typically above 4.8V). Note the voltage at rest and while slowly pressing the pedal.

fixSteps.title: Check Pedal Assembly Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Ensure the connector is fully seated. Disconnect and check for corrosion, bent pins, or contamination. Particularly inspect the ground pin for the sensor E circuit.

fixSteps.title: Test Circuit Integrity
fixSteps.instruction: Measure the ground path resistance for sensor E from the connector to battery ground. Check for continuity between the signal wire and 5V reference wire to rule out a short.

fixSteps.title: Repair Ground or Replace Pedal Assembly
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the ground connection if it is the cause. If the sensor itself has failed, replace the pedal assembly. Clear codes and verify proper sensor E voltage across the entire pedal range.

faq.q: Why does an open ground cause a high voltage reading?
faq.a: When the sensor loses its ground reference, current cannot flow through the sensor. The signal wire then floats toward the 5V reference voltage because there is no return path through the sensor element.

faq.q: Can corrosion on the connector cause P2128?
faq.a: Yes, corrosion on the ground pin can increase resistance enough to cause the signal voltage to rise above the maximum threshold, especially in vehicles exposed to salt, moisture, or flooding.

faq.q: Is P2128 more dangerous than P2127?
faq.a: Both are equally serious as they both indicate a loss of accurate pedal position data from sensor E. The PCM responds with the same safety measures regardless of whether the voltage is too high or too low.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch A/B Voltage Correlation
shortDescription: The two throttle position sensor signals (A and B) inside the throttle body do not agree with each other.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the two redundant throttle position sensors (TPS A and TPS B) inside the electronic throttle body are not tracking in the expected correlated pattern. In a drive-by-wire system, dual TPS sensors provide redundant position feedback for safety. If the sensors disagree by more than the allowed tolerance, the PCM cannot trust the throttle position data and will enter a fail-safe mode. This is one of the most common and important throttle-related safety codes.
safetyImpact: Critical safety code. Loss of throttle position sensor correlation means the PCM cannot verify throttle blade position. Vehicle enters fail-safe mode immediately.

causes.cause: Intermittent contact or wear in TPS A or TPS B inside the throttle body
Corroded throttle body connector affecting one sensor signal
Failed electronic throttle body with internal sensor degradation
Wiring harness damage affecting one TPS signal but not the other
5V reference voltage issue shared between sensors

symptoms: Check Engine Light with reduced power or limp mode
Intermittent or complete loss of throttle response
Engine stalls or will not accelerate
Electronic throttle control (ETC) warning light on dashboard
Vehicle may enter forced idle or RPM-limited mode

fixSteps.title: Monitor TPS A and B Live Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to simultaneously graph TPS A and TPS B voltages while slowly opening and closing the throttle. TPS A typically increases from about 0.5V to 4.5V while TPS B decreases from about 4.5V to 0.5V (or a similar complementary pattern). Note any dead spots or jumps where the signals diverge.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Throttle Body Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the throttle body connector and carefully inspect each terminal for corrosion, bent pins, or push-back. Clean the connector with electrical contact cleaner. Also check the mating connector on the harness side.

fixSteps.title: Check Reference Voltage and Ground
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the 5V reference voltage at the throttle body connector for both sensor circuits. Check the ground circuits for both sensors. A shared reference or ground issue could affect both sensors but in different ways.

fixSteps.title: Replace Throttle Body
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensors track independently and the wiring is good, the internal sensors in the throttle body have degraded and the throttle body must be replaced. After installation, perform the throttle body relearn procedure, clear codes, and verify TPS A/B correlation.

faq.q: Why are there two throttle position sensors?
faq.a: The dual TPS design is a critical safety feature in drive-by-wire systems. The PCM uses both sensors to verify the throttle blade position. If either sensor fails or they disagree, the PCM knows the data cannot be trusted and takes protective action.

faq.q: Is P2135 common?
faq.a: Yes, P2135 is one of the most commonly reported electronic throttle codes across all vehicle makes. It is frequently seen on GM, Toyota, Chrysler, and Volkswagen vehicles at higher mileages.

faq.q: Can a dirty throttle body cause P2135?
faq.a: Indirectly, yes. Carbon buildup can cause the throttle blade to bind at certain positions, which may cause one sensor to report a slightly different angle than the other, pushing them outside the correlation window.

title: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch D/E Voltage Correlation
shortDescription: The two accelerator pedal position sensor signals (D and E) do not agree with each other.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the dual accelerator pedal position (APP) sensors D and E are not tracking in the expected correlated pattern. Similar to P2135 for the throttle body, this code addresses the pedal assembly sensors. The two sensors should produce complementary or proportional signals as the pedal is pressed and released. When they diverge beyond the allowed tolerance, the PCM cannot determine the driver's actual throttle demand and enters fail-safe mode.
safetyImpact: Critical safety code. The PCM cannot determine the driver's intended throttle demand. Vehicle enters fail-safe mode.

causes.cause: Worn accelerator pedal position sensor contacts
Corroded pedal assembly connector pins
Intermittent wiring connection to one APP sensor
Mechanical wear in the pedal pivot affecting sensor alignment

symptoms: Check Engine Light with limp mode or reduced power
Vehicle limited to idle or very low speed
Engine may stall when accelerator is pressed
Electronic throttle control warning light illuminated

fixSteps.title: Monitor APP D and E Signals
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to graph APP Sensor D and E voltages simultaneously while slowly pressing and releasing the pedal. Note any positions where the signals diverge or one sensor shows erratic behavior.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Pedal Assembly and Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the accelerator pedal assembly for physical damage or wear. Check the electrical connector for corrosion, moisture, or loose terminals. Ensure the pedal pivot moves smoothly without binding.

fixSteps.title: Check Wiring to Both Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Test each wire from the pedal connector to the PCM for continuity and correct routing. Check that the 5V reference and ground circuits are shared properly or independently powered as designed.

fixSteps.title: Replace Accelerator Pedal Assembly
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor signals are not tracking correctly and the wiring is good, the pedal assembly sensors are worn and the entire assembly must be replaced. Clear codes and verify sensor D and E correlation through the full pedal range.

faq.q: Is P2138 the pedal version of P2135?
faq.a: Yes, exactly. P2135 addresses the correlation of the two throttle body position sensors, while P2138 addresses the correlation of the two accelerator pedal position sensors. Both are critical safety codes.

faq.q: Is the pedal assembly hard to replace?
faq.a: The accelerator pedal assembly is one of the easier throttle system components to replace. It is typically held in place by two or three bolts in the footwell with a single electrical connector.

faq.q: Can a floor mat interfere with the pedal sensors?
faq.a: A floor mat can restrict pedal movement which could cause abnormal sensor readings, but it would not typically cause a sensor correlation error. Still, always ensure floor mats are properly secured and do not interfere with pedal travel.

title: Fuel Injector Group A Supply Voltage Circuit Open
shortDescription: An open circuit has been detected in the power supply to fuel injector group A.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit in the supply voltage circuit that feeds fuel injector group A. In many engine designs, the fuel injectors are divided into groups (A and B) that share a common power supply circuit. When the supply circuit to group A has an open, the injectors in that group cannot fire, resulting in misfires on those specific cylinders. The engine will run very rough or may stall depending on how many cylinders are affected.
safetyImpact: Multiple cylinder misfires cause severe power loss and can make the vehicle unsafe to drive. Unburned fuel can damage the catalytic converter.

causes.cause: Blown fuel injector fuse or relay for group A
Open or broken wire in the injector supply harness
Corroded connector at the injector harness junction
PCM injector driver circuit failure for group A

symptoms: Severe engine misfire on multiple cylinders
Significant power loss or engine stalling
Strong raw fuel smell from exhaust
Rough idle or inability to idle

fixSteps.title: Check Injector Fuse and Relay
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the fuel injector fuse and relay in the engine bay fuse box. Test the fuse with a test light or multimeter. Swap the relay with a known-good identical relay to test. If the fuse is blown, check for a short before replacing.

fixSteps.title: Test Supply Voltage at Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: With the key on, use a test light or multimeter to verify 12V is present at each injector connector in group A. If no voltage is present, the supply wire between the fuse box and injectors is open.

fixSteps.title: Trace Supply Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: Follow the injector supply harness from the fuse box to the injector connectors. Check all intermediate connectors and splice points for opens. Use a multimeter to test continuity along the wire.

fixSteps.title: Repair Wiring or Replace Relay
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the blown fuse or failed relay. Repair any open wiring using proper soldering and heat-shrink techniques. If the PCM driver has failed, the PCM may need replacement. Clear codes and verify all injectors are firing.

faq.q: What is fuel injector group A?
faq.a: Manufacturers divide injectors into groups that share power supply circuits. Group A typically includes injectors for half of the cylinders. The specific cylinder assignment varies by vehicle.

faq.q: Can I drive with P2146?
faq.a: No, driving with an entire injector group not firing causes severe misfires, damages the catalytic converter with unburned fuel, and results in dangerous power loss. The vehicle should not be driven.

faq.q: Why did my injector fuse blow?
faq.a: A blown injector fuse is usually caused by a short circuit in the injector wiring harness or a failed injector with a shorted coil. The short must be found and repaired before replacing the fuse.

title: Fuel Injector Group B Supply Voltage Circuit Open
shortDescription: An open circuit has been detected in the power supply to fuel injector group B.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an open circuit in the supply voltage circuit for fuel injector group B. This is the counterpart to P2146 (group A) and affects the second group of fuel injectors. When the supply voltage to group B is lost, the injectors in that group cannot fire, causing misfires on those cylinders. Combined with a functional group A, the engine will run on only half its cylinders.
safetyImpact: Multiple cylinder misfires cause severe power loss. Unburned fuel will damage the catalytic converter and may cause a fire risk.

causes.cause: Blown fuel injector fuse or relay for group B
Open wire in the group B injector supply harness
Corroded harness connector feeding group B injectors
PCM driver circuit failure for group B

symptoms: Engine running on half the cylinders
Raw fuel smell from exhaust due to unburned fuel

fixSteps.title: Check Fuse and Relay for Group B
fixSteps.instruction: Identify and test the fuel injector group B fuse and relay. Some vehicles use a single relay for all injectors while others have separate circuits. Consult the fuse box diagram for the correct components.

fixSteps.title: Verify Supply Voltage at Group B Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Probe each injector connector in group B with a test light or multimeter, key on. If 12V is missing from all group B injectors but present at group A, the supply circuit is confirmed open.

fixSteps.title: Locate the Open Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Trace the supply wiring from the relay output to the group B injectors. Check all connectors, splice points, and pins along the path. Look for heat damage, chafing, or rodent damage to the harness.

fixSteps.title: Repair and Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Repair the open circuit in the harness, replace the failed fuse or relay, or address the PCM driver if faulty. Clear codes and verify all injectors fire correctly using a scan tool injector balance test or by monitoring misfire counters.

faq.q: Can having both P2146 and P2149 at the same time mean a common fault?
faq.a: Yes, if both groups lose power simultaneously, the cause is likely a shared power source such as the main fuel injection relay or a common ground that feeds both group circuits.

faq.q: How do I know which cylinders are in group B?
faq.a: The grouping varies by manufacturer and engine design. Consult your vehicle's service manual or wiring diagram to determine which specific cylinders belong to each injector group.

faq.q: Will running on half the cylinders damage the engine?
faq.a: Running briefly will not damage the engine, but the unburned fuel from the dead cylinders will contaminate the engine oil, wash down cylinder walls, and can overheat or melt the catalytic converter. Stop driving immediately.

title: Throttle Actuator Control System - Idle Position Not Learned
shortDescription: The PCM has not completed or cannot complete the electronic throttle body idle position learning procedure.
fullDescription: The PCM needs to learn the exact closed (idle) position of the electronic throttle blade to properly control idle speed. P2176 indicates that this learning procedure has not been completed or that the learned position is no longer valid. This typically occurs after the throttle body has been cleaned, replaced, or the battery has been disconnected, causing the PCM to lose its stored idle position reference.
safetyImpact: Idle speed may be higher or lower than normal, or unstable. Generally not a critical safety issue but can cause stalling at stops.

causes.cause: Throttle body relearn procedure not performed after cleaning or replacement
Battery disconnect or PCM power loss clearing learned values
Throttle body idle position has drifted due to carbon buildup
Intermittent power loss to PCM corrupting learned data

symptoms: Idle speed too high or too low
Engine may stall at idle, especially when coming to a stop
Rough transition from deceleration to idle

fixSteps.title: Perform Throttle Body Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: Using the OBD-II scanner, navigate to the throttle body relearn or idle relearn function. Follow the scan tool prompts to complete the procedure. This typically requires the engine to be at normal operating temperature with all accessories off.

fixSteps.title: Alternative Key Cycle Relearn
fixSteps.instruction: If a scan tool relearn is not available, many vehicles support a key cycle method: turn key on without starting, wait 10-15 seconds, turn off, wait 10-15 seconds, repeat 2-3 times, then start the engine and let it idle for 3-5 minutes.

fixSteps.title: Clean Throttle Body if Needed
fixSteps.instruction: If the idle position keeps drifting or cannot be learned, the throttle body may need cleaning. Remove the intake duct and clean the blade and bore. Then perform the relearn procedure again.

fixSteps.title: Check Battery and PCM Power
fixSteps.instruction: If the relearn value keeps being lost, check the battery health and PCM power supply circuits. A weak battery or intermittent PCM power connection can cause repeated loss of learned parameters.

faq.q: Can I just clear the code and keep driving?
faq.a: Clearing the code alone will not fix it. The throttle body relearn procedure must be performed for the PCM to establish the correct idle position reference. Driving without the relearn may result in an erratic idle.

faq.q: Why did this code appear after a battery replacement?
faq.a: When the battery is disconnected, the PCM loses its stored learned parameters, including the throttle body idle position. Performing the relearn procedure after any battery disconnect is recommended.

faq.q: Do I need a special scan tool for the relearn?
faq.a: Many basic OBD-II scan tools can initiate a throttle relearn. However, some vehicles require a manufacturer-specific or enhanced scan tool. The key cycle method is a free alternative that works on many vehicles.

title: System Too Lean Off Idle (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too lean during off-idle operating conditions (acceleration, cruise, load).
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the air-fuel mixture on bank 1 is too lean specifically during off-idle conditions such as part-throttle driving, acceleration, or steady cruise. This code is more specific than P0171 (general lean) because it pinpoints the lean condition to off-idle operation, which narrows the diagnostic focus to fuel delivery under load rather than idle air leaks. Common causes include insufficient fuel supply during higher demand or metered air leaks that are amplified under manifold pressure changes.
safetyImpact: Lean operation under load can cause engine overheating and detonation, potentially leading to engine damage if left unaddressed.

causes.cause: Weak fuel pump not meeting demand at higher loads
Partially clogged fuel filter restricting flow under load
Dirty or clogged fuel injectors on bank 1
Intake manifold gasket leak that worsens under vacuum changes
Faulty mass airflow sensor over-reporting airflow

symptoms: Pinging or knocking noise during acceleration

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Pressure Under Load
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a fuel pressure gauge and monitor pressure at idle and under load (during acceleration or wide-open throttle). Pressure should remain within specification under all conditions. A significant drop under load indicates a fuel delivery problem.

fixSteps.title: Inspect and Replace Fuel Filter
fixSteps.instruction: If the vehicle has a serviceable fuel filter, inspect and replace it. Many modern vehicles have the filter inside the fuel tank as part of the fuel pump assembly. Check the service manual for your vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Clean or Test Fuel Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Run a professional fuel injector cleaning treatment or have the injectors professionally flow-tested and cleaned. Compare the flow rates across all injectors on bank 1 for uniformity.

fixSteps.title: Test MAF Sensor and Check for Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the MAF sensor with MAF-specific cleaner. Compare MAF sensor readings to manufacturer specifications at various RPMs. Perform a smoke test on the intake system to find any leaks that open under load.

faq.q: What is the difference between P2177 and P0171?
faq.a: P0171 indicates a general lean condition at any operating point. P2177 specifically indicates lean operation during off-idle conditions (driving, acceleration), which points more toward fuel supply issues rather than idle vacuum leaks.

faq.q: Can a clogged catalytic converter cause P2177?
faq.a: A severely restricted catalytic converter can cause increased backpressure that affects fuel delivery and combustion, potentially contributing to lean conditions. However, it would typically trigger other codes as well.

faq.q: Should I replace the fuel pump?
faq.a: Only if fuel pressure testing confirms the pump cannot maintain adequate pressure under load. Many P2177 cases are resolved with a simple fuel filter replacement or injector cleaning rather than a full pump replacement.

title: System Too Rich Off Idle (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too rich during off-idle operating conditions.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an excessively rich air-fuel mixture on bank 1 during off-idle conditions such as part-throttle driving, acceleration, or cruise operation. This code indicates that more fuel than needed is being delivered during driving conditions, which can be caused by excessive fuel pressure, leaking injectors, or sensor errors that cause the PCM to over-fuel during load conditions.
safetyImpact: Rich operation under load causes elevated catalytic converter temperatures and excessive fuel consumption. Prolonged operation can damage the catalyst.

causes.cause: Leaking fuel injector(s) on bank 1 over-delivering fuel
Excessive fuel pressure from a faulty pressure regulator
Faulty mass airflow sensor under-reporting airflow
Faulty coolant temperature sensor causing rich bias
Saturated charcoal canister purging excess fuel vapor

symptoms: Black smoke from exhaust during acceleration
Spark plugs fouled with black soot

fixSteps.title: Monitor Fuel Trims Under Load
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor short-term and long-term fuel trims during a road test. Negative fuel trim values beyond -10% indicate the PCM is trying to compensate for a rich condition by reducing fuel delivery.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Install a fuel pressure gauge and monitor pressure at idle and under load. If pressure exceeds specification, the fuel pressure regulator may be stuck closed or the return line may be restricted.

fixSteps.title: Test MAF and Coolant Temperature Sensors
fixSteps.instruction: Compare MAF sensor readings to manufacturer specifications at various loads. Check coolant temperature sensor reading against actual engine temperature using an infrared thermometer. An ECT reading colder than actual causes rich fueling.

fixSteps.title: Service Injectors and Repair
fixSteps.instruction: Perform an injector leak-down test to identify any injectors that drip fuel when closed. Replace leaking injectors. If the pressure regulator is faulty, replace it. Clear codes and verify fuel trims return to normal.

faq.q: Can a dirty air filter cause P2178?
faq.a: A severely restricted air filter reduces airflow to the engine, which can cause a rich condition. Replacing the air filter is a simple and inexpensive first step in diagnosis.

faq.q: How does P2178 differ from P0172?
faq.a: P0172 indicates a general system-rich condition. P2178 specifically identifies the rich condition as occurring during off-idle (driving) conditions, which helps narrow the diagnosis to fuel pressure and injector issues rather than idle-specific problems.

faq.q: Will running rich damage my engine?
faq.a: Short-term rich operation is less harmful than lean operation, but prolonged rich running washes oil from cylinder walls, dilutes engine oil with fuel, fouls spark plugs, and can overheat the catalytic converter.

title: System Too Lean at Idle (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too lean specifically at idle speed.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a lean condition on bank 1 that occurs specifically at idle speed. This code targets idle operation, which distinguishes it from P0171 (general lean) and P2177 (lean off-idle). At idle, manifold vacuum is highest, making vacuum leaks most pronounced. A lean condition at idle typically indicates unmetered air entering the intake through vacuum leaks, a stuck-open PCV valve, or an idle air control issue.
safetyImpact: Engine may stall at idle, particularly when coming to a stop or in gear. Stalling can be hazardous in traffic.

causes.cause: Vacuum leak at the intake manifold gasket
Cracked or disconnected vacuum hose
Stuck-open PCV valve admitting excess air
Leaking brake booster diaphragm
Faulty idle air control valve or electronic throttle body at idle position

symptoms: Engine stalling at idle or when stopping
Higher than normal idle RPM as PCM compensates
Hissing or whistling noise from the engine bay at idle

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test for Vacuum Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a smoke test on the intake system with the engine off. Introduce smoke into the intake manifold and watch for smoke escaping from cracked hoses, gaskets, or the brake booster connection.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Vacuum Hoses and PCV
fixSteps.instruction: Visually inspect all vacuum hoses for cracks, splits, or disconnection. Pay special attention to small-diameter hoses that are easily overlooked. Check the PCV valve by shaking it; a working valve should rattle.

fixSteps.title: Check Brake Booster
fixSteps.instruction: With the engine running, pinch or block the brake booster vacuum hose. If the idle smooths out and fuel trims improve, the brake booster diaphragm is leaking and needs replacement.

fixSteps.title: Repair Leaks and Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Replace cracked hoses, failed gaskets, the PCV valve, or the brake booster as needed. Clear codes and monitor fuel trims at idle to confirm the lean condition is resolved. Long-term fuel trim should be within plus or minus 5%.

faq.q: Why is my car lean only at idle?
faq.a: At idle, the engine runs under high vacuum, which magnifies the effect of any air leaks. A small crack in a vacuum hose may not affect the mixture at higher RPMs when the throttle is open but causes a noticeable lean condition at idle.

faq.q: Can I use carb cleaner to find vacuum leaks?
faq.a: Yes, spraying small amounts of carb cleaner around suspected leak areas while the engine idles is a common diagnostic technique. If the idle speed changes when you spray an area, you have found a leak. Be cautious with flammable sprays near hot engine components.

faq.q: Is P2187 common on any specific vehicles?
faq.a: P2187 is particularly common on Volkswagen and Audi vehicles with the 2.0T FSI/TSI engine due to the PCV system and intake manifold gasket being common failure points on these engines.

title: System Too Rich at Idle (Bank 1)
shortDescription: The fuel system on bank 1 is running too rich specifically at idle speed.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected an excessively rich air-fuel mixture on bank 1 during idle conditions. This code identifies a rich condition that is specific to idle operation, distinguishing it from a general rich code. At idle, a rich condition may be caused by a leaking fuel injector that drips fuel when it should be closed, an incorrect idle air control setting, a contaminated MAF sensor reading low, or a coolant temperature sensor error.
safetyImpact: Rich idle can cause stalling due to spark plug fouling and increases the risk of catalytic converter damage from excessive unburned fuel.

causes.cause: Leaking fuel injector dripping fuel at idle
Contaminated or faulty MAF sensor under-reading at idle airflow
Coolant temperature sensor reading incorrectly cold
High fuel pressure at idle from a faulty regulator

fixSteps.title: Monitor Idle Fuel Trims
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to check short-term and long-term fuel trims at idle. Negative values beyond -10% indicate the PCM is removing fuel to compensate for a rich condition. Compare to fuel trims at 2500 RPM to confirm the problem is idle-specific.

fixSteps.title: Test for Leaking Injectors
fixSteps.instruction: Perform an injector leak-down test. With the fuel rail pressurized and engine off, observe the injector tips for any dripping. Even a single drop per minute can cause a rich idle condition.

fixSteps.title: Clean MAF Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and clean the MAF sensor using MAF-specific cleaner spray. A contaminated MAF sensor may under-report airflow at idle, causing the PCM to deliver more fuel than needed. Allow it to dry completely before reinstalling.

fixSteps.title: Check Coolant Temp Sensor and Fuel Pressure
fixSteps.instruction: Verify the coolant temperature sensor reading matches actual engine temperature. Check fuel pressure at idle with a gauge. Repair or replace any faulty components found. Clear codes and verify fuel trims normalize at idle.

faq.q: Can a leaking injector really cause P2188?
faq.a: Yes, even a very small amount of fuel leaking from an injector at idle adds up because the engine is consuming very little fuel at idle. A dripping injector that would be barely noticeable at highway speeds is very significant at idle.

faq.q: Why is my car rich at idle but normal when driving?
faq.a: At idle, fuel demand is very low, so any extra fuel from a leak or sensor error represents a large percentage of the total fuel delivery. At higher RPMs and load, the same amount of extra fuel is a much smaller percentage and may not trigger the code.

faq.q: Will P2188 affect my fuel economy?
faq.a: While the rich condition at idle itself wastes some fuel, the bigger impact on fuel economy is usually from the PCM's adaptive fuel trim corrections, which may not perfectly compensate across all operating conditions.

title: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
shortDescription: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently reading lean or is biased toward lean values.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the upstream (pre-catalyst) oxygen sensor on bank 1 is producing a signal that is consistently biased toward the lean side. The sensor voltage remains below the expected midpoint or does not switch to the rich side as often as expected during normal closed-loop fuel control. This may indicate a faulty O2 sensor or an actual lean condition that the fuel system is struggling to correct.
safetyImpact: If the PCM is adding excess fuel to compensate for a false lean reading, the engine may run rich, damaging the catalytic converter. If genuinely lean, engine damage from detonation is possible.

causes.cause: Aging or contaminated upstream O2 sensor with lean bias
Exhaust leak before the upstream O2 sensor
Genuine lean condition that exceeds fuel trim correction range
O2 sensor heater circuit malfunction causing slow warm-up

symptoms: Poor fuel economy (from PCM over-fueling to compensate)
Long-term fuel trim excessively positive (adding fuel)

fixSteps.title: Analyze O2 Sensor Waveform
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to graph the bank 1 sensor 1 O2 sensor signal. A healthy sensor should oscillate between approximately 0.1V and 0.9V. A lean-biased sensor will spend most of its time below 0.45V with infrequent or no rich excursions.

fixSteps.title: Check for Exhaust Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the exhaust manifold, header gaskets, and any joints upstream of the O2 sensor for leaks. An exhaust leak introduces ambient air that registers as a lean reading on the sensor.

fixSteps.title: Verify Actual Fuel Mixture
fixSteps.instruction: Check long-term fuel trims. If LTFT is very positive (over +15%), the engine may actually be lean. If LTFT is near zero but the sensor reads lean, the sensor itself is likely biased. Use a known-good wideband sensor for comparison if available.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor or Fix Lean Condition
fixSteps.instruction: If the sensor is biased, replace the upstream bank 1 O2 sensor with an OEM or quality aftermarket unit. If a genuine lean condition exists, address the root cause (vacuum leaks, fuel delivery). Clear codes and verify proper sensor switching after replacement.

faq.q: How do I know if the sensor is bad or the engine is actually lean?
faq.a: Check the long-term fuel trim. If LTFT is significantly positive (adding fuel), the engine may genuinely be lean. If LTFT is near normal but the sensor reads lean, the sensor is likely biased and needs replacement.

faq.q: Can I use a universal O2 sensor?
faq.a: Universal sensors can work if wired correctly, but OEM or direct-fit sensors are recommended for proper voltage output, heater circuit compatibility, and connector fit. Using the wrong sensor can cause ongoing issues.

faq.q: How often should O2 sensors be replaced?
faq.a: Most manufacturers recommend replacing O2 sensors every 60,000 to 100,000 miles as preventive maintenance, though they may fail sooner or last longer depending on fuel quality and driving conditions.

title: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Rich (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
shortDescription: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently reading rich or is biased toward rich values.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently producing a signal biased toward the rich side. The sensor voltage remains above the expected midpoint or does not switch to the lean side frequently enough during closed-loop fuel control. The PCM may be removing fuel to compensate, which could cause an actual lean condition if the sensor is falsely reading rich.
safetyImpact: If the PCM reduces fuel based on a false rich reading, the engine could actually run lean, causing potential detonation and engine damage.

causes.cause: Aging O2 sensor with rich bias due to contamination
Silicon contamination from coolant leak or improper sealant
O2 sensor wiring shorted to voltage source
Genuine rich condition beyond fuel trim correction range

symptoms: Engine may run lean if PCM overcompensates (hesitation, misfires)
Negative long-term fuel trim values (PCM removing fuel)

fixSteps.title: Graph O2 Sensor Signal
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to graph the bank 1 sensor 1 voltage over time. A rich-biased sensor will spend most of its time above 0.45V with infrequent or no lean excursions below 0.2V.

fixSteps.title: Check Fuel Trims
fixSteps.instruction: Review long-term fuel trims. If LTFT is very negative (beyond -15%), the PCM is aggressively removing fuel, suggesting the sensor is reading rich. If LTFT is near zero, a genuine rich condition may be present.

fixSteps.title: Inspect for Contamination Sources
fixSteps.instruction: Check for coolant leaks (head gasket, intake manifold) that could contaminate the sensor with silicon. Verify no silicone-based sealants were used on the exhaust system. Inspect the sensor tip for white or green deposits indicating contamination.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the upstream bank 1 O2 sensor. If contamination from coolant is found, address the coolant leak first to prevent contaminating the new sensor. Clear codes and verify proper sensor oscillation and normalized fuel trims.

faq.q: What causes an O2 sensor to become rich-biased?
faq.a: The most common causes are age-related degradation of the sensing element, silicon contamination from coolant leaks or silicone sealants, and oil contamination from an engine that burns oil.

faq.q: Can a rich-biased sensor cause engine damage?
faq.a: Indirectly, yes. If the PCM reduces fuel delivery based on a false rich reading, the engine may actually run lean under load, which can cause detonation, overheating, and piston damage.

faq.q: Should I replace both upstream sensors at the same time?
faq.a: While not strictly necessary, if the vehicle has high mileage and one sensor has failed, the other is likely nearing the end of its life as well. Replacing both at the same time can prevent a return visit.

title: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
shortDescription: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 is consistently reading lean or is biased toward lean values.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 is consistently producing a lean-biased signal. This is the bank 2 equivalent of P2195. On V-type engines, this sensor is located in the exhaust manifold or downpipe of the cylinder bank that does not contain cylinder 1. The lean bias may indicate a faulty sensor, an exhaust leak on bank 2, or a genuine lean condition affecting bank 2 cylinders.
safetyImpact: Engine may run rich on bank 2 as the PCM compensates for the perceived lean condition, potentially damaging the bank 2 catalytic converter.

causes.cause: Worn or contaminated bank 2 upstream O2 sensor
Exhaust manifold leak on bank 2
Vacuum leak affecting primarily bank 2 cylinders
O2 sensor heater failure causing inaccurate readings when cold

symptoms: Bank 2 long-term fuel trim excessively positive
Possible rough running or misfires on bank 2 cylinders

fixSteps.title: Compare Bank 1 and Bank 2 Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to compare O2 sensor waveforms and fuel trims between bank 1 and bank 2. If only bank 2 shows lean bias, the issue is isolated to that side.

fixSteps.title: Check Bank 2 Exhaust for Leaks
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the bank 2 exhaust manifold, header-to-pipe connection, and all gaskets for signs of exhaust leaks. Look for soot marks, listen for ticking at cold start, and check for loose bolts or cracked manifolds.

fixSteps.title: Test O2 Sensor Response
fixSteps.instruction: Create a momentary rich condition by briefly restricting airflow at idle. The bank 2 sensor 1 should quickly respond with a voltage spike above 0.8V. A slow or muted response indicates a sluggish or biased sensor.

fixSteps.title: Replace Sensor or Repair Exhaust Leak
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the bank 2 upstream O2 sensor if it is biased. Repair any exhaust leaks found. If a vacuum leak is the cause, replace the affected hoses or gaskets. Clear codes and verify bank 2 fuel trims normalize.

faq.q: Why is bank 2 lean but not bank 1?
faq.a: Bank-specific lean readings usually point to a localized issue: an exhaust manifold crack on bank 2, a vacuum leak near bank 2 cylinders, or the bank 2 O2 sensor itself being faulty.

faq.q: Can I just replace the sensor without finding the root cause?
faq.a: If the sensor is genuinely biased due to age, replacing it is the correct fix. However, if there is an actual lean condition or exhaust leak, replacing only the sensor will result in the code returning.

faq.q: Is the bank 2 sensor harder to access?
faq.a: On many V6 and V8 engines, bank 2 (the rear bank on transverse engines) can be more difficult to access. You may need to work from underneath or remove intake components to reach the sensor.

title: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
shortDescription: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently reading lean or is stuck at a lean voltage.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the downstream (post-catalyst) oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently producing a lean-biased signal. The downstream sensor normally shows a relatively steady voltage near 0.6-0.7V after the catalytic converter reaches operating temperature. A persistent lean reading may indicate a faulty sensor, an exhaust leak between the catalyst and the sensor, or a deteriorating catalytic converter that is affecting the downstream sensor readings.
safetyImpact: Post-catalyst sensor issues primarily affect emissions monitoring. Driving safety is minimally impacted but the vehicle will fail emissions testing.

causes.cause: Faulty downstream O2 sensor with lean bias
Exhaust leak between catalytic converter and rear O2 sensor
Deteriorating catalytic converter affecting exhaust composition
O2 sensor wiring issue on the signal circuit

symptoms: Possible subtle decrease in fuel economy
No noticeable drivability symptoms in most cases

fixSteps.title: Monitor Rear O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor the bank 1 sensor 2 voltage over time at operating temperature. It should show a steady reading around 0.5-0.7V. A reading consistently below 0.3V indicates a lean bias.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Post-Catalyst Exhaust
fixSteps.instruction: Check the exhaust pipe and joints between the catalytic converter outlet and the rear O2 sensor for leaks. Even a small leak here introduces ambient air that registers as lean.

fixSteps.title: Compare with Upstream Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Compare the behavior of the upstream and downstream O2 sensors. If the upstream sensor is functioning normally but the downstream shows lean, the issue is either the downstream sensor, a post-cat exhaust leak, or the catalyst.

fixSteps.title: Replace Downstream O2 Sensor
fixSteps.instruction: If an exhaust leak is not found, replace the downstream bank 1 O2 sensor. Use penetrating oil on the sensor threads as they are often seized in the exhaust pipe. Clear codes and allow the catalyst monitor to complete to verify the fix.

faq.q: Will P2270 cause drivability problems?
faq.a: In most cases, P2270 does not cause noticeable drivability issues because the downstream O2 sensor is primarily used for catalyst monitoring, not fuel control. However, on some vehicles the PCM may use the rear sensor for fine fuel trim adjustments.

faq.q: Is P2270 the same as a bad catalytic converter?
faq.a: Not necessarily. P2270 indicates the sensor is reading lean, which can be caused by the sensor itself, an exhaust leak, or a failing catalyst. P0420 is the more specific code for catalyst efficiency below threshold.

faq.q: Can I pass emissions with P2270?
faq.a: No, any illuminated Check Engine Light will cause an automatic failure on OBD-II emissions inspections. The code must be repaired and the catalyst monitor must complete successfully to pass.

title: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Rich (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
shortDescription: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently reading rich or is stuck at a rich voltage.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is consistently producing a rich-biased signal, staying above the expected midpoint voltage. The post-catalyst sensor should normally produce a relatively steady mid-range voltage. A persistent rich reading can indicate a contaminated or failing sensor, a catalytic converter that is not properly oxidizing hydrocarbons, or an actual rich exhaust condition that the catalyst cannot fully process.
safetyImpact: Primarily an emissions issue. Minimal direct safety impact but the vehicle will fail emissions testing and catalyst health may be degraded.

causes.cause: Aging or contaminated downstream O2 sensor with rich bias
Catalytic converter not fully oxidizing exhaust hydrocarbons
Engine running rich overwheling the catalytic converter capacity
Oil contamination on the O2 sensor from engine burning oil

symptoms: Possible slight fuel odor from exhaust
No obvious drivability problems in most cases
Long-term fuel trim may be affected on some vehicles

fixSteps.title: Read Rear O2 Sensor Voltage
fixSteps.instruction: Monitor bank 1 sensor 2 voltage at operating temperature. A rich-biased sensor will consistently read above 0.7V when it should be near 0.5-0.7V with minimal fluctuation.

fixSteps.title: Check for Rich-Running Engine
fixSteps.instruction: Review fuel trims and front O2 sensor data. If the engine is running rich (negative fuel trims, front sensor biased rich), the rich exhaust is overwhelming the catalyst, causing the rear sensor to read rich legitimately.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Sensor for Contamination
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the downstream O2 sensor and inspect the tip. Black soot indicates a rich exhaust. White or oily deposits suggest coolant or oil contamination. Replace if contaminated.

fixSteps.title: Replace O2 Sensor and Address Root Cause
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the downstream sensor if it is biased. If the engine is running rich, address that issue first (leaking injectors, fuel pressure, etc.) before replacing the sensor to prevent contaminating the new one. Clear codes and verify.

faq.q: Is P2271 more serious than P2270?
faq.a: Both are similar in severity. P2271 (rich bias) can indicate that the catalyst is not properly oxidizing fuel, which may be a sign of catalyst degradation that P2270 (lean bias) does not typically indicate.

faq.q: Can engine oil cause the downstream sensor to read rich?
faq.a: Yes, if the engine burns oil, the oil vapors pass through the catalytic converter and can contaminate the downstream O2 sensor, causing it to read rich.

faq.q: Do I need to replace the catalytic converter?
faq.a: Not necessarily. P2271 often points to the sensor itself or an upstream rich condition. Only if catalyst efficiency codes like P0420 are also present should you consider the converter as the problem.

title: Intake Air System Leak
shortDescription: The PCM has detected a significant leak in the intake air system between the mass airflow sensor and the throttle body or intake manifold.
fullDescription: The PCM has determined that there is a significant air leak in the intake air system, specifically in the area between the mass airflow (MAF) sensor and the engine intake. Air entering the system after the MAF sensor is unmetered, meaning the PCM does not account for it in fuel calculations, resulting in a lean condition. This code is specifically designed to detect intake leaks rather than relying on general lean fuel trim codes.
safetyImpact: An intake leak causes lean operation which can lead to rough running, potential engine overheating, and reduced power at critical moments.

causes.cause: Cracked or disconnected intake air duct between MAF and throttle body
Loose or missing clamp on the intake air duct
Torn or deteriorated intake duct boot
Cracked intake manifold or loose manifold bolts
Failed intake manifold gasket

symptoms: Whistling or hissing noise from the engine bay

fixSteps.title: Visual Inspection of Intake Ducting
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the entire intake air path from the air filter box to the throttle body. Check all rubber boots, connections, and clamps. Look for cracks, tears, or disconnected sections. Squeeze and flex the ducts while looking for hidden cracks.

fixSteps.title: Smoke Test
fixSteps.instruction: Perform a smoke test by introducing smoke into the intake system with the engine off. Watch for smoke escaping from cracked ducts, loose connections, or failed gaskets. This is the most reliable method for finding intake leaks.

fixSteps.title: Check Intake Manifold
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect the intake manifold for cracks, especially on plastic manifolds common in modern vehicles. Verify that all manifold mounting bolts are tight and that the gasket is not deteriorated or improperly seated.

fixSteps.title: Repair and Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Replace cracked ducts or boots, tighten loose clamps, or replace the intake manifold gasket as needed. Clear codes and monitor fuel trims at idle and off-idle to verify the leak has been eliminated.

faq.q: How can I hear an intake leak?
faq.a: With the engine running, listen carefully near the intake ducting for a hissing or whistling sound, especially at idle. The sound may change pitch when the throttle is blipped. This sound indicates air being drawn through a crack or gap.

faq.q: Can a loose air filter cause P2279?
faq.a: A loose or improperly installed air filter can allow unfiltered air to bypass the filter and enter through gaps, but it typically does not cause a large enough unmetered air leak to trigger P2279 unless the filter box is significantly unsealed.

faq.q: Why is the leak between the MAF and throttle body the problem area?
faq.a: Air that enters after the MAF sensor is not measured by the sensor, so the PCM does not know about it and cannot adjust fuel delivery. Air entering before the MAF is measured and accounted for in fuel calculations.

title: Brake Pedal Position/Accelerator Pedal Position Incompatible
shortDescription: The PCM has detected that both the brake pedal and accelerator pedal are being pressed simultaneously in an incompatible manner.
fullDescription: The PCM monitors both the brake pedal position switch/sensor and the accelerator pedal position sensor. P2299 is set when both pedals indicate significant application simultaneously for longer than the expected threshold. This is a safety feature designed to detect situations where the brake and throttle are in conflict, which could indicate a stuck accelerator pedal, a faulty brake switch, or driver error. The PCM typically prioritizes the brake pedal and reduces engine power.
safetyImpact: Simultaneous brake and throttle application is a safety concern. The PCM will override throttle input and prioritize braking to prevent unintended acceleration.

causes.cause: Faulty brake pedal position switch stuck in the on position
Misadjusted brake light switch
Accelerator pedal physically sticking or not returning fully
Floor mat interfering with pedal travel

symptoms: Reduced power or throttle restriction during braking
Brake lights staying on when the pedal is released
Vehicle hesitates when accelerating after braking
Cruise control may not engage or may disengage unexpectedly

fixSteps.title: Check Brake Switch Status
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to monitor the brake switch status in real time. Release the brake pedal completely and verify the switch shows the off position. If it still reads on, the switch needs adjustment or replacement.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Brake Light Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the brake light switch on the brake pedal bracket. Check that it is properly positioned and that the pushrod or plunger contacts the pedal arm correctly. Also verify that the brake lights turn off when the pedal is fully released.

fixSteps.title: Check Pedal Free Play
fixSteps.instruction: Verify that both the brake and accelerator pedals return to their full rest positions freely. Check for floor mat interference, worn pedal pivots, or anything obstructing full pedal return travel.

fixSteps.title: Adjust or Replace Brake Switch
fixSteps.instruction: Adjust the brake light switch position so it properly releases when the brake pedal is not pressed. If the switch is faulty, replace it. On some vehicles, the switch clips directly onto the pedal bracket and self-adjusts upon installation. Clear codes and test drive.

faq.q: Is P2299 a serious safety concern?
faq.a: The code itself indicates a safety system is working as designed by detecting a brake/throttle conflict. The underlying cause should be addressed promptly, but the PCM's brake override function is protecting you in the meantime.

faq.q: Can two-foot driving cause P2299?
faq.a: Drivers who habitually rest their left foot on the brake pedal (two-foot driving) can trigger this code if they inadvertently apply enough brake pressure while accelerating.

faq.q: Will P2299 affect my cruise control?
faq.a: Yes, the brake switch is used to disengage cruise control. If the brake switch is stuck on, cruise control will not engage or will immediately disengage, in addition to triggering P2299.

title: Ignition Coil A Secondary Circuit - Insufficient Ionization
shortDescription: The ignition coil for cylinder A is not producing sufficient spark energy as measured by the ionization detection circuit.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected that ignition coil A is not producing adequate spark energy based on the ionization current sensing feedback. Modern ignition systems on some vehicles use ion-sensing technology to measure combustion quality by detecting the ionization current that flows through the spark plug gap after the spark event. Insufficient ionization indicates weak spark, combustion failure, or a failing coil that cannot sustain the arc long enough for proper combustion.
safetyImpact: A weak ignition coil causes misfires which reduce power and can damage the catalytic converter. Sudden misfire during acceleration may affect vehicle control.

causes.cause: Failing or weak ignition coil A with degraded output
Worn or fouled spark plug increasing required firing voltage
Damaged ignition coil connector or terminal corrosion
Excessive spark plug gap beyond coil capability

symptoms: Engine misfire on the associated cylinder

fixSteps.title: Identify the Affected Cylinder
fixSteps.instruction: Scan for all codes and note which cylinder is associated with coil A for your specific vehicle. Check for associated misfire codes (P0301-P0312) that correspond to the same cylinder.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the spark plug for the affected cylinder and inspect its condition. Check the electrode gap and compare to specifications. Look for fouling, wear, cracking, or carbon deposits that would increase firing voltage requirements.

fixSteps.title: Test Ignition Coil
fixSteps.instruction: Swap the suspected coil with a known-good coil from another cylinder. If the insufficient ionization code follows the coil, it is confirmed faulty. Also measure the coil primary and secondary resistance with a multimeter.

fixSteps.title: Replace Coil and Spark Plug
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the faulty ignition coil and the spark plug for that cylinder. Apply dielectric grease to the coil boot to prevent moisture intrusion. Clear codes and test drive to verify no misfires occur.

faq.q: What is ionization sensing?
faq.a: Ionization sensing uses the spark plug as a sensor after the spark event. The combustion flame creates ions that conduct a small current across the plug gap. The PCM analyzes this current to evaluate combustion quality without a separate sensor.

faq.q: Should I replace all ignition coils at once?
faq.a: While not strictly necessary, if one coil has failed and the others are original with high mileage, the remaining coils may be nearing failure as well. Replacing them as a set prevents repeated repairs.

faq.q: Can a bad spark plug cause an ignition coil to fail?
faq.a: Yes, a worn spark plug with an excessively wide gap or high resistance can force the coil to produce higher voltage than designed, which accelerates coil insulation breakdown and can lead to premature coil failure.

title: EVAP Emission System Leak Detection Pump Control Circuit Low
shortDescription: The voltage in the EVAP leak detection pump control circuit is below the minimum expected level.
fullDescription: The PCM has detected a low voltage condition in the control circuit for the EVAP system leak detection pump (LDP). The LDP is used in some vehicles to pressurize or create a vacuum in the EVAP system to detect fuel vapor leaks. A low voltage in the control circuit prevents the pump from operating, which means the EVAP system cannot perform its required leak test. This will cause the EVAP monitor to fail to complete.
safetyImpact: No direct safety impact. This is an emissions system monitoring code. The vehicle is safe to drive but will fail emissions testing.

causes.cause: Short to ground in the LDP control circuit wiring
Failed leak detection pump motor with shorted windings
Corroded LDP connector pins
PCM LDP driver circuit failure

symptoms: EVAP system readiness monitor does not complete
Failed emissions inspection due to incomplete monitor
Possible fuel vapor smell if EVAP system is not sealed

fixSteps.title: Locate Leak Detection Pump
fixSteps.instruction: Find the EVAP leak detection pump, which is typically located near the fuel tank, charcoal canister, or in the engine bay depending on the vehicle. Consult the service manual for the exact location on your vehicle.

fixSteps.title: Inspect LDP Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect the LDP electrical connector and inspect for corrosion, moisture, or damaged pins. Check that the connector is properly sealed against water intrusion since the pump is often in an exposed location.

fixSteps.title: Test LDP Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: With the connector disconnected, check the control circuit wire for a short to ground using a multimeter. Also verify that the PCM is providing the correct control signal by checking voltage at the connector with the key on.

fixSteps.title: Replace LDP or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the pump motor is shorted, replace the leak detection pump assembly. If the wiring has a short to ground, locate and repair it. Clear codes and allow the EVAP monitor to run through its complete test cycle to verify the repair.

faq.q: Can I pass emissions without the EVAP monitor?
faq.a: Most states allow one or two monitors to be incomplete (not ready) and still pass. However, the EVAP monitor is one that some states specifically require to be complete. Check your state's requirements.

faq.q: What does the leak detection pump do?
faq.a: The LDP creates a slight pressure or vacuum in the fuel tank and vapor recovery system. The PCM then monitors whether the pressure holds to detect even tiny leaks that would allow fuel vapors to escape into the atmosphere.

faq.q: Is this related to the gas cap?
faq.a: While both relate to the EVAP system, P2401 is specifically about the electrical circuit to the leak detection pump, not a physical leak. A loose gas cap would trigger P0442 or P0456 instead.

title: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detection Pump Control Circuit High
shortDescription: The PCM detected that voltage on the leak detection pump control circuit is higher than expected.
fullDescription:
P2402 is set when the Powertrain Control Module detects that the voltage on the EVAP leak detection pump (LDP) control circuit remains high when it should be pulled low. This usually indicates an open circuit, a failed pump, or a PCM driver problem.

The LDP is used on many Chrysler, VW, and Audi vehicles to pressure-test the EVAP system. When the PCM cannot command the pump properly, it cannot complete its leak check, and other EVAP monitors may also fail to run.
safetyImpact: No immediate safety impact. The vehicle will run normally, but it will fail emissions testing and may release fuel vapor to the atmosphere.

causes.cause: Open circuit in LDP control wiring
Failed leak detection pump
Corroded LDP connector
Faulty PCM driver

fixSteps.title: Scan and Record
fixSteps.instruction: Connect a scan tool and record all freeze-frame data and any other stored codes before clearing.

fixSteps.title: Inspect LDP Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Locate the leak detection pump (usually near the fuel tank or charcoal canister) and inspect the connector for corrosion, bent pins, or water intrusion.

fixSteps.title: Test Control Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Back-probe the LDP control wire and measure voltage with key on. Compare to the service manual spec. An always-high reading with no PCM command indicates an open or shorted wire.

fixSteps.title: Replace Pump or Repair Wiring
fixSteps.instruction: If the control wire is good, replace the LDP. Otherwise repair the wiring and clear codes.

faq.q: Will this affect drivability?
faq.a: No, you should not notice any change in how the car drives. The main consequence is an illuminated Check Engine Light and failed emissions testing.

title: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The EVAP leak detection pump sense circuit is reporting values outside expected range.
fullDescription:
P2404 indicates the PCM has detected that the sense (feedback) circuit of the EVAP leak detection pump is not reporting values consistent with pump operation. The pump may be running, but the feedback signal is not tracking correctly.

This code commonly appears on Chrysler and VW products that use a reed-switch type LDP. It can also be caused by small mechanical issues inside the pump such as a stuck diaphragm or worn reed switch.
safetyImpact: No drivability impact. Emissions compliance is affected.

causes.cause: Worn LDP reed switch
Partially blocked EVAP hoses
Wiring high resistance
Faulty PCM

fixSteps.title: Verify Fault
fixSteps.instruction: Drive the vehicle through a complete drive cycle to verify P2404 is an active, not pending, fault.

fixSteps.title: Inspect EVAP Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Inspect all EVAP hoses between the tank, canister, and LDP for kinks or crush damage.

fixSteps.title: Test Sense Circuit
fixSteps.instruction: Using a scope or bidirectional scan tool, command the LDP on and watch the sense signal. It should pulse as the pump cycles.

fixSteps.title: Replace LDP
fixSteps.instruction: If the pump cycles but the sense signal is dead or stuck, replace the LDP assembly.

faq.q: Can I just replace the reed switch?
faq.a: On most LDPs the reed switch is integrated into the pump housing and not serviced separately. You typically replace the entire pump.

title: Exhaust Gas Recirculation System Performance
shortDescription: The EGR system is not achieving the commanded flow rate.
fullDescription:
P2413 is set when the PCM commands the EGR valve to a certain position and monitors the resulting change in manifold pressure or intake air temperature, but the change does not match expectations. This indicates restricted flow, a stuck valve, or sensor feedback error.

Carbon buildup is the most common cause, especially on vehicles with over 100,000 miles. The code can also trigger if the EGR cooler is clogged or if downstream passages are restricted.
safetyImpact: May cause hesitation under load and increased NOx emissions. Severe carbon buildup can lead to engine damage over time.

causes.cause: Carbon buildup in EGR passages
Stuck EGR valve
Clogged EGR cooler
Faulty EGR position sensor

fixSteps.title: Read Live EGR Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to view commanded vs actual EGR position and MAP change during a light-load drive.

fixSteps.title: Remove and Inspect EGR Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the EGR valve and inspect the pintle, seat, and passages for carbon buildup.

fixSteps.title: Clean Passages
fixSteps.instruction: Clean the valve and intake passages with carburetor cleaner and a soft brush. Do not use sharp tools that could damage sealing surfaces.

fixSteps.title: Reinstall and Verify
fixSteps.instruction: Install a new gasket, reinstall the valve, clear codes, and confirm the EGR monitor runs successfully.

faq.q: Can I drive with P2413?
faq.a: Short distances are fine, but prolonged driving with EGR problems can increase combustion temperatures and accelerate carbon buildup on other components.

title: EVAP Switching Valve Control Circuit Open
shortDescription: The PCM detected an open circuit in the EVAP switching valve control wiring.
fullDescription:
P2418 is set when the PCM cannot detect continuity through the EVAP switching valve control circuit. The switching valve directs purge flow between different parts of the EVAP system and is critical for proper leak testing.

Common on European vehicles. Often caused by connector corrosion or a broken wire near the valve.
safetyImpact: No drivability impact, but EVAP system cannot complete self-tests.

causes.cause: Open circuit in control wire
Failed switching valve solenoid
Corroded connector
PCM driver fault

fixSteps.title: Locate Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Consult the service manual to locate the EVAP switching valve - often near the charcoal canister or fuel tank.

fixSteps.title: Check Connector
fixSteps.instruction: Disconnect and inspect the connector for corrosion or bent pins. Clean with electrical contact cleaner if needed.

fixSteps.title: Measure Solenoid
fixSteps.instruction: Measure resistance across the solenoid terminals. Compare to service manual spec (typically 20-40 ohms).

fixSteps.title: Repair or Replace
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the valve if the solenoid is open, or repair wiring if continuity to PCM is lost.

faq.q: Will my car fail emissions?
faq.a: Yes, until the EVAP monitor runs and passes, most states will fail the vehicle.

title: Secondary Air Injection System Air Flow/Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
shortDescription: The secondary air injection pressure/flow sensor is out of expected range.
fullDescription:
P2431 is set when the sensor that monitors secondary air pump output reports pressure or flow values outside the expected window. The secondary air system pumps fresh air into the exhaust manifold on cold starts to help the catalytic converter reach operating temperature faster.

Most common on BMW, Mercedes, and Audi vehicles. Often caused by rust or carbon blocking the combination valve downstream of the pump.
safetyImpact: No drivability impact under normal driving. Cold-start emissions may be slightly elevated.

causes.cause: Clogged combination valve
Weak secondary air pump
Faulty pressure sensor
Exhaust leak before sensor

symptoms: Pump runs longer than normal at cold start

fixSteps.title: Observe Cold Start
fixSteps.instruction: Listen for the secondary air pump at cold start - it should run for about 60 seconds.

fixSteps.title: Check Sensor Data
fixSteps.instruction: Use a scan tool to read the secondary air pressure PID while the pump runs.

fixSteps.title: Inspect Combination Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove the combination valve and check for rust, carbon, or stuck diaphragm.

fixSteps.title: Replace Faulty Part
fixSteps.instruction: Replace the combination valve, pump, or sensor depending on diagnosis.

faq.q: Can I bypass the secondary air system?
faq.a: No - it is part of the emissions system and bypassing it is illegal in most jurisdictions and will fail inspection.

title: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve Stuck Open Bank 1
shortDescription: The secondary air injection switching valve on bank 1 is stuck in the open position.
fullDescription:
P2440 indicates the PCM has detected that the bank 1 secondary air injection switching valve is not closing properly after the cold-start period. This allows exhaust gases to flow backward into the secondary air pump, damaging it.

Very common on late-model BMW, Audi, and Porsche vehicles. Rust from exhaust condensation is typically the cause.
safetyImpact: No immediate drivability issue, but can destroy the expensive secondary air pump if not addressed.

causes.cause: Rusted/stuck switching valve
Broken vacuum line to valve
Failed vacuum solenoid

fixSteps.title: Inspect Vacuum Lines
fixSteps.instruction: Check vacuum hoses from the intake to the switching valve for cracks or disconnection.

fixSteps.title: Test Valve Operation
fixSteps.instruction: Apply vacuum to the valve manually and confirm it opens and closes freely.

fixSteps.title: Replace Switching Valve
fixSteps.instruction: Remove and replace the switching valve. Use anti-seize on the exhaust fasteners.

fixSteps.title: Clear and Verify
